question: Two charged particles of mass $1 g$ each are placed $1 m$ apart. If each of these possesses 1 femto coulomb of charge, then the dominant force of interaction between them is
explanation:
masses $m_{1}=m_{2}=10^{-3} {~kg}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952799/preptracker/questions/q_111856_explanation_1764952799305.png $\therefore$ Gravitational force between them,
$F_{G}=\dfrac{G m_{1} m_{2}}{r^{2}}=\dfrac{6.67 \times 10^{-11} \times 10^{-3} \times 10^{-3}}{1^{2}}$
$\left[\therefore G=6.67 \times 10^{-11} {~N} \cdot {~m}^{2} / {kg}^{2}\right]$
$F_{G}=6.67 \times 10^{-17} N$
$q_{1}=q_{2}=1$ femto coulomb $=10^{-15} {C}$
$\therefore$ Electrostatic force between the both charges,
$F_{e}=\dfrac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \dfrac{q_{1} q_{2}}{r^{2}}=9 \times 10^{9} \times \dfrac{10^{-15} \times 10^{-15}}{l^{2}}$
$F_{e}=9 \times 10^{-21} N$
$\therefore$ Clearly $F_{G} \gg F_{{e}}$
Hence, gravitational force is dominant force.
subject: Physics
subtopic: Force between two charges

question: Two charges $+10 \mu C$ and $-10 \mu C$ are separated by $10\,cm$. The magnitude of force acting on another charge $5 \mu {C}$ placed at the mid-point of the line joining the two charges will be(use, $\dfrac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}=9 \times 10^{9}$ in $S I$ unit)
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952808/preptracker/questions/q_111854_explanation_1764952807602.png The magnitude of force on charge, $q_{3}=5 \mu C$ placed at mid-point $O$ is given as
$F=\dfrac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \dfrac{q_{1} q_{3}}{(A O)^{2}}+\dfrac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \dfrac{q_{1} q_{2}}{(O B)^{2}}$
$=9 \times 10^{9} \times \dfrac{10 \times 10^{-6} \times 5 \times 10^{-6}}{\left(5 \times 10^{-2}\right)^{2}}$
$ + 9 \times {10^9} \times \frac{{5 \times {{10}^{ - 6}} \times 10 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}}}{{{{\left( {5 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}} \right)}^2}}}$
$ = \frac{{45 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}}{{25 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}}} + \frac{{45 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}}{{25 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}}}$
$ = \frac{9}{5} \times {(10)^2} + \frac{9}{5} \times {(10)^2} = \frac{{18}}{5} \times {(10)^2}$
$ = 3.6 \times {10^2}N = 360N$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Force between Multiple Charges

question: A thin metallic spherical shell of radius $r$ contains a charge $Q$ on its surface. A point charge $q_{1}$ is placed at the centre of shell and another charge $q_{2}$ is placed outside the shell at a distance $x$ from the centre. Then, the forces on charges $q_{1}$ and $q_{2}$ respectively, are
explanation:
Given situation is https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952798/preptracker/questions/q_111848_explanation_1764952797935.png As field inside shell is zero due to charge on the metallic shell & out side the shell, so force on $q_{1}$ is $F_{1}=q_{1} E_{\text {in }}=0$
The force on $q_{2}$ is due to both $q_{1}$ and $Q$.
$F=\dfrac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \dfrac{q_{2} Q}{x^{2}}+\dfrac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \dfrac{q_{2} q_{1}}{x^{2}}$
$F=\dfrac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \dfrac{q_{2}\left(Q+q_{1}\right)}{x^{2}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Force between Multiple Charges

question:
Two small conducting balls of identical mass
$20\,g$ and identical charge $10^{-10} {C}$ hang from non-conducting threads of length, $L=300 {~cm}$. If the equilibrium separation of balls is $x$ and $x \ll L$ then the magnitude of $x$ is (Assume, $4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}=\dfrac{1}{9 \times 10^{9}} {~F} / {m}$ and $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$)
explanation:
According to the question, https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952791/preptracker/questions/q_111841_explanation_1764952791315.png Equilibrium at point $B$,
$T\cos \theta = mg\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (1)$
$T\sin \theta = F$ $ \Rightarrow T\sin \theta = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}} \cdot \frac{{{q^2}}}{{{x^2}}}\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (2)$
From Eqn. (1) and (2), we get
$\dfrac{T \sin \theta}{T \cos \theta}=\dfrac{\dfrac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \dfrac{q^{2}}{x^{2}}}{m g} \Rightarrow m g \tan \theta=\dfrac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \dfrac{q^{2}}{x^{2}}$
From $\triangle A B M$, for small angles $(x \ll L)$
$\tan \theta \simeq \sin \theta=\dfrac{x}{2 L}$
$m g \cdot \dfrac{x}{2 L}=\dfrac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \dfrac{q^{2}}{x^{2}}$
Putting the given values, we get
$2 \times 10^{-2} \times 10 \times \dfrac{x}{2 \times 3}=\dfrac{9 \times 10^{9} \times 10^{-20}}{x^{2}}$
$\Rightarrow x^{3}=\dfrac{27}{10^{10}}$
$\Rightarrow \quad x=\dfrac{3}{10^{3} \times 10^{1 / 3}} m=\dfrac{3}{10^{1 / 3}} m m$
Therefore, magnitude of equilibrium separation distance of balls is $\dfrac{3}{10^{1 / 3}} {~mm}$.
subject: Physics
subtopic: Equilibrium of system of charges

question: The ratio of electric force to gravitational force between two particles having charges $q_{1}, q_{2}$ and masses $m_{1}$ and $m_{2}$ respectively is (where symbols have their usual meanings)
explanation:
$F_{g}=\dfrac{G m_{1} m_{2}}{r^{2}}$ Gravitational force
$F_{e}=\dfrac{q_{1} q_{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r^{2}} \quad$ Electrical force
\begin{equation*}\dfrac{F_{e}}{F_{g}}=\dfrac{q_{1} q_{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} G m_{1} m_{2}}\end{equation*}

question: Force between two point charges $q_{1}$ and $q_{2}$ placed in vacuum at '$r$'   $cm$ apart is $F$. Force between them when placed in a medium having dielectric constant $k=5$ at  '$r / 5$'  $cm$  apart will be
explanation:
Given: $F = \frac{{{q_1}{q_2}}}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}{r^2}}}\quad \quad (1)$
When point charges $q_{1}$ and $q_{2}$ placed in medium with dielectric constant $k=5$ and
$r' = \frac{r}{5},{\rm{ then }}$
$F' = \frac{{{q_1}{q_2}}}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}{{r'}^2}k}} = \frac{{{q_1}{q_2}}}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0} \times 5{{\left( {\frac{r}{5}} \right)}^2}}} = 5\,F$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Force between two charges

question: Two identical conducting spheres $P$ and $S$ with charge $Q$ on each, repel each other with a force $16\,N$ A  third identical uncharged conducting sphere $R$ is successively brought in contact with the two spheres. The new force of repulsion between $P$ and $S$ is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955526/preptracker/questions/q_32097_explanation_1764955525925.png
${F_{PS}} = 16N = K \cdot \frac{{{Q^2}}}{{{r^2}}}\quad \quad (1)$
When $P$ and $R$ are in contact,
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955526/preptracker/questions/q_32097_explanation_1764955526556.png
Charge on $P=\dfrac{Q}{2}=$ Charge on $R$
( $\because$ Identical conducting bodies have half of total charge when touches each other)
Again $R$ and $S$ are in contact,
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955527/preptracker/questions/q_32097_explanation_1764955527443.png
$\therefore$ Charge on $S=\dfrac{3 Q}{4}$
Since the distance between sphere $P$ and $S$ does not change, the new force $\left(F^{\prime}{ }_{P S}\right)$ between the spheres is,
${{F'}_{PS}} = K\frac{{{Q_P} \cdot {Q_S}}}{{{r^2}}} = K \cdot \frac{{\frac{Q}{2} \cdot \frac{{3Q}}{4}}}{{{r^2}}}$
${{F'}_{PS}} = \frac{{K \cdot Q \times 3Q}}{{8 \times {r^2}}} = \frac{3}{8} \cdot \frac{{K{Q^2}}}{{{r^2}}}$
Using equation (1),
${F'_{PS}} = \frac{3}{8} \times 16 = 6\,N\quad \quad ({\rm{from}}(1))$

subtopic: Force between two charges

question: In hydrogen like system, the ratio of coulombian force and gravitational force between an electron and a proton is in the order of
explanation:
For electron and proton,
${m_e} = 9.1 \times {10^{ - 31}}\,kg,{m_p} = 1.67 \times {10^{ - 27}}\,kg,$
${q_p} = 1.6 \times {10^{ - 19}}C = {q_e}$
Coulombian force between an electron and a proton,
$F_{C}=\dfrac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{o}} \times \dfrac{q_{e} q_{p}}{r^{2}}=\dfrac{9 \times 10^{9} \times\left(1.6 \times 10^{-19}\right)^{2}}{r^{2}}$
$=\dfrac{2.304 \times 10^{-28}}{r^{2}} N$
Gravitational force between an electron and a proton,
$F_{G}=\dfrac{G m_{e} m_{p}}{r^{2}}$
$=\dfrac{6.67 \times 10^{-11} \times\left(9.1 \times 10^{-31}\right) \times 1.67 \times 10^{-27}}{r^{2}} N$
$=\dfrac{1.01 \times 10^{-67}}{r^{2}} N$
$\therefore \dfrac{F_{C}}{F_{G}}=\dfrac{2.304 \times 10^{-28}}{1.01 \times 10^{-67}}=2.28 \times 10^{39}$
So, option (3) is correct.

subtopic: Force between two charges

question: A point charge ${Q}$ is fixed at the centre of an insulated disc of mass ${M}$. The disc rests on a rough horizontal plane. Another charge having same value as point charge is fixed vertically above the centre of the disc at a height of ${h}$. After the disc is displaced slightly in the horizontal direction (friction is sufficient to prevent slipping). If the period of oscillation of disc is found to be ${2 \pi \dfrac{h}{Q} \sqrt{n \pi \varepsilon_{0} M h}}$,
explanation:
Let the radius of the disc be $R$. If the disc is displaced $x$, the corresponding angular displacement $\theta=x / R$.
The restoring torque $\tau$ about the point of contact of disc with ground,
${\tau _p} = (F\sin \theta )R{\kern 1pt} $
$(F\sin \theta )$$R = I\alpha = \left[ {\frac{{M{R^2}}}{2} + M{R^2}} \right]\alpha \quad \quad (1)$
where $F = \frac{{{Q^2}}}{{4\,\pi {\varepsilon _0}\left( {{h^2} + {x^2}} \right)}}\quad \quad (2)$
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955518/preptracker/questions/q_32013_explanation_1764955518055.png
and $\sin \theta = \frac{x}{{\sqrt {{h^2} + {x^2}} }}\quad \quad (3)$
From (1), (2) and (3), we get
$\frac{{{Q^2}xR}}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}{{\left( {{h^2} + {x^2}} \right)}^{3/2}}}} = \left[ {\frac{{M{R^2}}}{2} + M{R^2}} \right]\alpha {\rm{ }}$
${\rm{or }}\,\,\alpha = \frac{{{Q^2}x}}{{6\pi {\varepsilon _0}MR{{\left( {{h^2} + {x^2}} \right)}^{3/2}}}}{\rm{ }}$
${\rm{For}}{\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} x < < h,\alpha \simeq - \frac{{{Q^2}x}}{{6\pi {\varepsilon _0}MR{h^3}}}$
${\rm{and }}\,\,\alpha = \frac{a}{R} \simeq - \frac{{{Q^2}x}}{{6\pi {\varepsilon _0}MR{h^3}}}$
Negative sign is being introduced because angular acceleration and angular displacement are opposite to each other.
Thus, $a=-\dfrac{Q^{2} x}{6 \pi \varepsilon_{0} M h^{3}}$
Hence, $\omega=\sqrt{\dfrac{Q^{2}}{6 \pi \varepsilon_{0} M h^{3}}}$
or $\quad T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{6 \pi \varepsilon_{0} M h^{3}}{Q^{2}}}=2 \pi \dfrac{h}{Q} \sqrt{6 \pi \varepsilon_{0} M h}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Force between two charges

question: Two identical beads each having a mass ${m}$ and charge ${q}$. When placed in a hemispherical bowl of radius ${R}$ with frictionless, non-conducting walls, the beads move, and at equilibrium they are a distance ${R}$ apart (see figure). If the charge on each bead is found to be ${q=\left[\left(\dfrac{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} m g R}{\sqrt{N}}\right)^{1 / 2}\right]}$, find the value  of ${N}$.
explanation: Figure shows FBD of ball $B$. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764949894/preptracker/questions/q_31962_explanation_1764949894091.png For equilibrium of ball $B$,we use Lami's theorem which gives$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{m g}{\sin \left(90+30^{\circ}\right)}=\dfrac{F}{\sin \left(90^{\circ}+60^{\circ}\right)} \\\Rightarrow \quad & F=\dfrac{m g}{\sqrt{3}} \\\Rightarrow \quad & \dfrac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \dfrac{(q)(q)}{R^{2}}=\dfrac{m g}{\sqrt{3}}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Force between two charges

question: Two identical small equally charged conducting balls are suspended from long threads secured at one point. The charges and masses of the balls are such that they are in equilibrium when the distance between them is ${a}$ (the length of thread $L >  > a$ ). One of the balls is then discharged. Again for the certain value of distance ${b(b <  < l)}$ between the balls the equilibrium is restored,  find  the value of ${\dfrac{a^{3}}{b^{3}}}$ is
explanation:
$T\sin \theta = F = \frac{1}{{4\pi { \in _0}}}\frac{{{q^2}}}{{{a^2}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
$T\cos \theta = mg\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
From (1) and (2), ${\tan \theta=\dfrac{q^{2}}{4 \pi \epsilon_{0} a^{2} m g}}$
$\frac{{a/2}}{L} = {\rm{ }}\frac{{{q^2}}}{{4\pi { \in _0}{a^2}mg}}$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} (\because {\text{ for small }}\theta ,\tan \theta \approx a/2L)$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} {\text{ }}\frac{{{a^3}}}{L} = \frac{{{q^2}}}{{2\pi {_0}mg}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 3 \right)$
When one of the ball is discharged, the balls come closes and touch each other and again separate due to repulsion. The charge on each ball after touching each other ${=q / 2}$. Replacing ${q}$ with ${q / 2}$ in (3), we get
$\frac{{{b^3}}}{L} = {\rm{ }}\frac{{{{(q/2)}^2}}}{{2\pi { \in _0}mg}}{\rm{ }}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \left( 4 \right)$
From (3) and (4) ${\dfrac{b^{3}}{a^{3}}=\dfrac{1}{4} \Rightarrow \dfrac{a^{3}}{b^{3}}=4}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Force between two charges

question: A small ball of mass ${10^{ - 3}}\,kg$ having a charge of $1\,\mu C$ is suspended by a string of length $1.0\,m$. Another identical ball having the same charge is kept at the point of suspension. The minimum horizontal velocity which should be imparted to the lower ball, so that it can make complete revolution is____  $m/s$. (Take $\sqrt{10}=3.16$ )
explanation:
$q = {10^{ - 6}}C$,
${m}=10^{-3} {~kg}, l=1.0 {~m}$
Let $u$ be the speed of particle at its lowest point and $v$ its speed at highest point. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955509/preptracker/questions/q_31894_explanation_1764955509148.png At highest point,
$\dfrac{m v^{2}}{l}=m g-F_{e}$
${v}^{2}=\dfrac{l}{{~m}}\left({mg}-\dfrac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \dfrac{{q}^{2}}{l^{2}}\right)$
$=\dfrac{1}{10^{-3}}\left(10^{-3} \times 9.8-\dfrac{9 \times 10^{9} \times\left(10^{-6}\right)^{2}}{(1)^{2}}\right)$
$=(9.8-9)$
$v^{2}=0.8 {~m}^{2} / {s}^{2}$
From conservation of energy.
Increase in GPE = Decrease in KE
$\therefore {mg}(2 l)=\dfrac{1}{2} {~m}\left({u}^{2}-v^{2}\right)$
$\therefore u^{2}=v^{2}+4 g l$
$\therefore u^{2}=0.8+4 \times 9.8 \times 1$$=40$
$\therefore {u}=2 \sqrt{10}$
$=2 \times 3.16$
$=6.32 {~m} / {s}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Force between two charges

question: Two point charges $ + 3\,\mu C$ and $ + 8\,\mu C$ repel each other with a force of 40 $N$ . If a charge of $ - 5\,\mu C$ is added to each of them, then the force between them will become
explanation:
${ F=\dfrac{k q_{1} q_{2}}{r^{2}}}$
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955507/preptracker/questions/q_31859_explanation_1764955507057.png ${40=k \dfrac{q_{1} q_{2}}{r^{2}} \Rightarrow r^{2}=\dfrac{k(3 \times 8)}{40}}$
If ${-5 C}$ is added to each charge
${F^{\prime}=k \dfrac{\left[q_{1}+(-5)\right]\left[q_{2}+(-5)\right]}{r^{2}}}$
${=k \dfrac{[3-5][8-5]}{r^{2}}=\dfrac{k(-2) \times(3)}{k \times 3 \times 8} \times 40}$
${F^{\prime}=-10 {~N}}$.
So correct option is (1)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Force between two charges

question: Charge $Q$ is distributed to two different metallic spheres having radii $R$ and $2\,R$ such that both spheres have equal surface charge density, then charge on large sphere is
explanation:
Let $q$ and $q'$ be the charges on spheres of radii $R$ and $2 R$ respectively.
Given $q + {q^\prime } = Q\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Surface charge densities are
$\sigma = \frac{q}{{4\pi {R^2}}}\,{\rm{ and}}\,{\rm{ }}\sigma = \frac{{{q^\prime }}}{{4\pi {{(2R)}^2}}}$
Given $\sigma = \sigma '$
$\therefore \frac{q}{{4\pi {R^2}}} = \frac{{{I^\prime }}}{{4\pi {{(2R)}^2}}}{\rm{ }}\,{\rm{or}},{\rm{ }}\,{q^\prime } = 4q$
From eq. (1), $q^{\prime}=Q-q$ or, $4 q=Q-q$
or, $Q = 5q\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
$\therefore {q^\prime } = Q - q = Q - \frac{Q}{5} = \frac{{4Q}}{5}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Force between two charges

question: Charges $4 Q, q$ and $Q$ are placed along $x$-axis at position $x=0, x=l / 2$ and $x=l$, respectively. Find the value of $q$, so that force on charge $Q$ is zero.
explanation:
From Coulomb's law, the force acting between two charges $\left(q_{1}, q_{2}\right)$
separated at a distance $r$ is given by
$F=\dfrac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \dfrac{q_{1} q_{2}}{r^{2}}$
Total force acting on charge $Q$ is
$F=\dfrac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \dfrac{q Q}{(l / 2)^{2}}+\dfrac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \dfrac{4 Q \cdot Q}{(l)^{2}}$
According to question, $F=0$
$\begin{aligned}& \therefore \quad \dfrac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \dfrac{q Q}{(l / 2)^{2}}+\dfrac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \dfrac{4 Q^{2}}{(l)^{2}}=0
\\& \Rightarrow \quad q=-Q\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Force between Multiple Charges

question: A and $B$ are two identical spherical charged bodies, which repel each other with force $F$, kept at a finite distance. A third uncharged sphere of the same size is brought in contact with sphere $B$ and removed. It is then kept at mid-point of $A$ and $B$. Find the magnitude of force on $C$.
explanation:
From Coulomb's law, the force of attraction
between two charged particles $(q)$, kept at distance $r$ apart is
$F=\dfrac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \dfrac{q^{2}}{r^{2}}$
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952991/preptracker/questions/q_31788_explanation_1764952991230.png
When two identical spheres are brought in contact,
then charge on them is equalised,
hence total charge on $C$ is equally shared when brought in contact with sphere $B$ having a charge $q$.
Therefore, charge on $B$ and $C$ is $\dfrac{q}{2}$.
From Coulomb's law, the force on $C$ is
$\begin{aligned}F_{C} & =\dfrac{q \times q / 2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}(r / 2)^{2}}-\dfrac{(q / 2)(q / 2)}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}(r / 2)^{2}}
\\& =\dfrac{q q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r^{2}}(2-1)=F\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Force between Multiple Charges

question: If charge and distance between two charges are reduced to half, then force between them is
explanation:
As per Coulomb's law, $(F = k\frac{{{q_1}{q_2}}}{{{r^2}}})$
According to question,
${F^\prime } = k\frac{{\left( {\frac{{{q_1}}}{2}} \right)\left( {\frac{{{q_2}}}{2}} \right)}}{{{{\left( {\frac{r}{2}} \right)}^2}}} = k\frac{{{q_1}{q_2}}}{{{r^2}}} = F$
Thus, force between them will remains same.
subject: Physics
subtopic: Force between two charges

question: Two spherical conductors $B$ and $C$ having equal radii and carrying equal charges in them repel each other with a force $F$ when kept apart at some distance. A third spherical conductor having same radius as that of $B$ but uncharged is brought in contact with $B$, then brought in contact with $C$ and finally removed away from both. The new force of repulsion between $B$ and $C$ is
explanation:
Let the spherical conductors $B$ and $C$ have same charge as $q$.
The electric force between them is
$F=\dfrac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \dfrac{q^{2}}{r^{2}}$
where, $r$ being the distance between them.
When third uncharged conductor $A$ is brought in contact with $B$, then charge on each conductor
${q_A} = {q_B} = \frac{{{q_A} + {q_B}}}{2}$
$ = \frac{{0 + q}}{2} = \frac{q}{2}$
When this conductor $A$ is now brought in contact with $C$, then charge on each conductor
${q_A} = {q_C} = \frac{{{q_A} + {q_C}}}{2}$
$ = \frac{{(q/2) + q}}{2} = \frac{{3\,q}}{4}$
Hence, electric force acting between $B$ and $C$ is
$F' = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{{{q_B}{q_C}}}{{{r^2}}} = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{{(q/2)(3q/4)}}{{{r^2}}}$
$ = \frac{3}{8}\left[ {\frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{{{q^2}}}{{{r^2}}}} \right] = \frac{{3F}}{8}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Force between two charges

question: The distance between charges $5 \times {10^{ - 11}}C$ and $ - 2.7 \times {10^{ - 11}}C$ is $0.2\,m$. The distance, at which third charge should be placed from second charge in order that it will not experience any force along the line joining the two charges, is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764949885/preptracker/questions/q_31756_explanation_1764949884739.png
Let a charge $q$ is placed at a point $C$ such that force at $C$ is zero.
${F_{{q_2}}} = $ force at $q$ due to charge $q_{2}$
and ${F_{{q_1}}} = $ force at $q$ due to charge $q_{1}$.
${\text{As}},\,\,\,{F_{{q_1}}} + {F_{{q_2}}} = 0$
$ \Rightarrow {F_{{q_1}}} = - {F_{{q_2}}}$
or $\left| {{F_{{q_1}}}} \right| = - \left| {{F_{{q_2}}}} \right|$
$\Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \dfrac{q_{1} q}{(x+0.2)^{2}}=\dfrac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \dfrac{q q_{2}}{x^{2}}$
$\dfrac{5 \times 10^{-11}}{(x+0.2)^{2}}=\dfrac{2.7 \times 10^{-11}}{x^{2}}$
$x = 0.556\,\,m$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Force between Multiple Charges

question: Three charges each of magnitude $q$ are placed at the corners of an equilateral triangle, the electrostatic force on the charge placed at the centre is (each side of triangle is $L$ )
explanation:
In the following figure, since $\left| {{F_A}} \right| = \left| {{F_B}} \right| = \left| {{F_C}} \right|$ and they are equally inclined with each other, so their resultant will be zero.
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955503/preptracker/questions/q_31752_explanation_1764955503375.png
${F_A} = {F_B}\cos 30^\circ + {F_C}\cos 30^\circ $ and
${F_B}\sin 30^\circ = {F_C}\sin 30^\circ $

question: Two identical conducting balls $A$ and $B$ have positive charges $q_{1}$ and $q_{2}$ respectively but $q_{1} \neq q_{2}$. The balls are brought together so that they touch each other and then kept in their original positions. The force between them is
explanation:
According to Coulomb's law, the force of repulsion between them is given by $(F = \frac{{{q_1}{q_2}}}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}{r^2}}})$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764949883/preptracker/questions/q_31748_explanation_1764949883424.png When the charged spheres $A$ and $B$ are brought in contact, each sphere will attain equal charge ${q'}$
$q' = \frac{{{q_1} + {q_2}}}{2}$
Now, the force of repulsion between them at the same distance $r$ is
${F^\prime } = \frac{{{q^1} \times {q^1}}}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}{r^2}}} = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}$
$\frac{{\left( {\frac{{{q_1} + {q_2}}}{2}} \right)\left( {\frac{{{q_1} + {q_2}}}{2}} \right)}}{{{r^2}}} = \frac{{{{\left( {\frac{{{q_1} + {q_2}}}{2}} \right)}^2}}}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}{r^2}}}{\rm{ }}$
As, ${\left( {\frac{{{q_1} + {q_2}}}{2}} \right)^2} > {q_1}{q_2}$
$\therefore \quad \quad {F^\prime } > F$

options.text: less than that before the balls touched
greater than that before the balls touched
same as that before the balls touched

question: If point charges ${Q_1} = 2 \times {10^{ - 7}}C\,\,{\text{and }}{Q_2} = 3 \times {10^{ - 7}}C$ are at $30 \mathrm{~cm}$ separation, then find electrostatic force between them.
explanation:

$ = 6 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,{\rm{N}}$

question:
If two charges $q_{1}$ and $q_{2}$ are separated by distance
'$d$' and placed in a medium of dielectric constant $k$. What will be the equivalent distance between charges in air for the same electrostatic force?
explanation:
Force between two charges in the medium
$F_{m}=\dfrac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} k} \dfrac{q_{1} q_{2}}{d^{2}}$
Force in vacuum is $F_{a}=\dfrac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \dfrac{q_{1} q_{2}}{d_{0}^{2}}$
Given, ${F_m} = {F_v};$
$\frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}k}}\frac{{{q_1}{q_2}}}{{{d^2}}} = \frac{{{q_1}{q_2}}}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}kd_0^2}}$
$\Rightarrow d_{0}^{2}=k d^{2} \Rightarrow d_{0}=\sqrt{k} d$
So option (4) is correct.
subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: If a conducting medium is placed between two charges, then electric force between them becomes zero.

Reason: Reduction in a force due to introduced material is inversely proportional to its dielectric constant. conductor is not dielectric.
explanation:
Such metals, $\epsilon_{r}=\infty$
$F^{\prime}=\left(F / \epsilon_{r}\right) \rightarrow 0$
But this is due to $F^{\prime}=\dfrac{1}{\epsilon_{r}}$
So correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

question:
Assertion: A charge is lying at the centre of the line joining two similar charges each which are fixed. The system will be in equilibrium if that charge is one fourth of the similar charges.

Reason: For charge to be in equilibrium, sum of the forces on charge due to rest of the two charges must be zero.
explanation:
If centre - charge is not present, original charges (given to be similar, say: $+Q$ and $+Q$ ) will have coulombic repulsion force between them. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955503/preptracker/questions/q_31523_explanation_1764955502575.png In order to nullify it and keep system in equilibrium, the centre - charge that to be placed shall be of different sign (here $q$ shall be negative).
$\dfrac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{0}}\left[\dfrac{Q Q}{d^{2}}\right]=\dfrac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{0}}\left[\dfrac{Q(-q)}{(d / 2)^{2}}\right]$
$\Rightarrow|q|=\left|\dfrac{Q}{4}\right| \Rightarrow q=(-Q / 4)$
For the charges to be in equilibrium, the vector sum of the forces due to the rest of the two charges must be zero. So correct option is (3).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

question:
Assertion: Range of Coulomb force is infinite.

Reason: Coulomb force acts between two charged particles.
explanation: The Coulomb force, which describes the electrostatic interaction between charged particles, theoretically extends to an infinite range because it decreases with the square of the distance between the charges. The reason is also true because Coulomb's law describes the force between two charged particles whether between $( + q{\rm{ and }} - q)$ or ($+q$ and $+-q$) or ($-q$ and $-q)$. Therefore, both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is not for assertion. So correct option is (2).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

question: A charge particle $q_{1}$ is at position $(2,-1,3)$. The electrostatics force on another charged particle $q_{2}$ at $(0,0,0)$ is :
explanation:

$\vec{F}_{21}=\dfrac{1}{4 \pi \in_{0}} \dfrac{q_{1} q_{2}}{r_{21}^{3}} \vec{r}_{21}$
where $\vec{r}_{21}$ is position vector of 2 with respect to 1.
So $\vec{F}_{21}=\dfrac{1}{4 \pi \in_{0}} \dfrac{q_{1} q_{2}}{(\sqrt{14})^{3}}(-2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-3 \hat{k})$

question: Charges on two spheres are $ + 10\,\mu C$ and $ - 5\,\mu C$ respectively. They experience a force $F$. If each of them is given an additional charge $ + 2\,\mu C$ then new force between them keeping the same distance is
explanation:
When there is no additional charge
$\begin{aligned}& F=\dfrac{K(+10)(5)}{R^{2}} \\& F=\dfrac{-50 K}{R^{2}}\end{aligned}$
when $+2 \mu C$ charge is added to both spheres.
Charge on sphere $1^{s t}=10 \mu C+2 \mu C=12 \mu C$
Charge on sphere $2^{\text {nd }}=-5 \mu C+2 \mu C=-3 \mu C$
New force will be $F^{\prime}=\dfrac{K(+12)(-3)}{R^{2}}=-\dfrac{36 K}{R^{2}}$
$\therefore \dfrac{F^{\prime}}{F}=-\dfrac{36 K}{R^{2}} \times \dfrac{R^{2}}{-50 K} \Rightarrow \dfrac{F^{\prime}}{F}=\dfrac{18}{25}$

question: Two identical metal spheres possesses $ + 60\,C$ and $ - 20\,C$ of charges. They are brought in contact and the then separated by $10\,cm$. The force between them is
explanation:

$q_{1}+q_{2}=60-20=40 C$
The two spheres will get equal share, i.e., $20 C$ each.
The force between the charges is
$F = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{{(20)(20)}}{{{{(0.1)}^2}}}$
$ = \frac{{9 \times {{10}^9} \times 400}}{{{{10}^{ - 2}}}} = 36 \times {10^{13}}\,N$

explanation:

$F=m a$
$F=q E$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{a_{e}}{a_{p}}=\dfrac{F_{e} / m_{e}}{F_{p} / m_{p}}=\dfrac{m_{p}(e E)}{m_{e}(e E)}=\dfrac{m_{p}}{m_{e}}$

question:

Force of any charge due to a number of other charges is the vector sum of all the forces on that charge due to other charges.
explanation: Force on any charge due to a number of other charges is the vector sum of all the forces on that charge due to the other charges. The individual forces are  unaffected due to the  presence of other charges. This is the principle  of superposition of charges. So option (3) is  correct.

${q_A} = Q - \frac{Q}{4} = \frac{{3Q}}{4}$ and ${q_B} = - Q + \frac{Q}{4} = \frac{{ - 3Q}}{4}$

${F_2} = \frac{{9{F_1}}}{{16}}$

question: Two identical conducting balls $A$ and $B$ have positive charges ${q_1}$ and ${q_2}$  respectively. But ${q_1} \ne {q_2}$.The balls are brought together so that they touch each other and then kept in their original positions. The force between them is
explanation:

question: The force of repulsion between two identical positive charges when kept with a separation $r$ in air is $F$. Half the gap between the two charges is filled by a dielectic slab of dielectric constant 16.Then the new force of repulsion between those two charges becomes
explanation:
Let $q$ be each charge,
According to Coloumb's law, the force of repulsion between the two charges when kept in air is
$F = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{{{q^2}}}{{{r^2}}}$
When half of the gap between the two charges is filled with the dielectric slab of dielectric constant $(K = 16)$, the new force of repulsion between them becomes
$F' = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{{{q^2}}}{{((r - t) + t\sqrt {K{)^2}} }}$
(where $t$ is the thickness of the slab)
$ = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{{{q^2}}}{{{{\left[ {\left( {r - \frac{r}{2}} \right) + \frac{r}{2}\sqrt {16} } \right]}^2}}}$
$\left( {{\rm{as}}\,\,t = \frac{r}{2},K = 16} \right)$
$\frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{{{q^2}}}{{{{\left( {\frac{r}{2} + 2r} \right)}^2}}} = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{{{q^2}}}{{{{\left( {\frac{5}{2}r} \right)}^2}}} = \frac{{4F}}{{25}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Force between two charges

question: Two small spheres of masses ${M_1}\,{\rm{and}}\,{M_2}$ are  suspended by weightless insulating threads of lengths ${L_1}\,{\rm{and}}\,{L_2}$ . The spheres carry charges ${Q_1}\,{\rm{and}}\,{Q_2}$ respectively. The spheres are  suspended such that they are in level with one  another and the threads are inclined to the  vertical at angles of ${\theta _1}\,{\rm{and}}\,{\theta _2}$ as shown in figure.  Which one of the following conditions is  essential, if ${\theta _1}\, = \,{\theta _2}?$
explanation:
The three forces acting on each sphere are;
${\rm{i) Tension}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\rm{ii)}}\,{\rm{Weight}}$
${\rm{iii)Electrostatic}}\,\,\,{\rm{force}}\,\,\,{\rm{of}}\,\,\,{\rm{repulsion}}$
For sphere 1
In equilibrium, from figure,
${T_{1\,}}\cos {\theta _1} = {M_1}g;\,{T_1}\sin {\theta _1} = {F_1}$
$\therefore \,\,\tan {\theta _1} = \frac{{{F_1}}}{{{M_1}g}}$
For sphere 2
In equilibrium, from figure
${T_{2\,}}\cos {\theta _1} = {M_2}g;\,{T_2}\sin {\theta _2} = {F_2}$
$\therefore \,\tan {\theta _2} = \frac{{{F_2}}}{{{M_2}g}}$
Force of repulsion between two charges are same.
$\therefore \,\,{F_1} = {F_2}$
${\theta _1} = {\theta _2} = $ only if
$\frac{{{F_1}}}{{{M_1}g}} = \frac{{{F_2}}}{{{M_2}g}}$
But ${F_1} = {F_2},$ then ${M_1} = {M_2}$

question: An isolated charge ${q_1}$ of mass $m$ is suspended freely by a thread of length $l$ . Another charge ${q_2}$ is brought near it $\left( {r >  > l} \right)$.When  ${q_1}$  is in equilibrium, tension in thread will be

question: Force between two identical charges placed at a distance of $r$ in vacuum is $F$. Now a slab of dielectric of dielectric contrant 4 is inserted between these two charges. If the thickness of the slab is $r/2$, then the force between the charges will become
explanation:
In vacuum, $F = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{{{q^2}}}{{{r^2}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Suppose, force between the charges is same when charges are $r'$distance apart in dielectric. $\therefore F' = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{{{q^2}}}{{kr{'^2}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
From (1) and (2) $kr{'^2} = {r^2}$ or, $r = \sqrt {kr'} $
In the given situation, force between the charges would be
$F' = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{{{q^2}}}{{{{\left( {\frac{r}{2} + \sqrt 4 \frac{r}{2}} \right)}^2}}} = \frac{4}{9}\frac{{{q^2}}}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}{r^2}}} = \frac{{4F}}{9}$

explanation: The dielectric constant for metal is infinity, the force between the two charges would be reduced to zero.

question: Two identical charged spheres of material density $\rho $, suspended from the same point by inextensible strings of equal length make an angle $\theta $ between the strings. When suspended in a liquid of density $s$ the angle  $\theta $  remains the same. The dielectric constant $K$ of the liquid is
explanation:
Initially, the forces acting on each sphere are as follows :
i) Weight, $W = mg$
ii) Tension in the string, $T$
iii) Electrostatic force of repulsion, $F$
For equilibrium of sphere
$T\sin \left( {\frac{\theta }{2}} \right) = F\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
and $T\cos \left( {\frac{\theta }{2}} \right) = mg\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
Divide (1) by (2), we get
$\tan \left( {\frac{\theta }{2}} \right) = \frac{F}{{mg}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(3)$
When they are suspended in a liquid of density $s$ and dielectric constant $K$, the angle $q$ and hence the distance between the spheres remains the same. Therefore,
Force of repulsion will become
$F' = \frac{F}{K}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(4)$
and weight will become
$mg' = mg - vsg$
$ = mg - \frac{m}{\rho }\sigma g = mg\left( {1 - \frac{\sigma }{\rho }} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(5)$
Let $T$ be the new tension in string.
For equilibrium of sphere,
$T'\sin \left( {\frac{\theta }{2}} \right) = F'\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(6)$
$T'\cos \left( {\frac{\theta }{2}} \right) = mg'\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(7)$
Divide (6) by (7), we get
$\tan \left( {\frac{\theta }{2}} \right) = \frac{{F'}}{{mg'}} = \frac{{F/K}}{{mg\,\left( {1 - \frac{\sigma }{\rho }} \right)}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(8)$
(Using (4) and (5))
From equations (3) and (8), we get
$K = \frac{1}{{\left( {1 - \frac{\sigma }{\rho }} \right)}} = \frac{\rho }{{(\rho - \sigma )}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Force between two charges

question: Four charges are arranged at the corners of a square $ABCD$ as shown in figure. The force on the charge kept at the centre $O$ is
explanation: The resultant field due to charge placed at $A$ and $C$ is zero. Field due to charge $( + 2q)$  will be along the direction of $OD$. Field due to charge $( - 2q)$ will be along the direction $OD$. The net force on the unit positive charge will be along $BD$.

question: Two point charges exert on each other a force $F$ when they are placed $r$ distnce apart in air. If they are placed $R$ distance apart in a medium of dielectric constant $K$, they exert double the  force. The distance $R$ equals
explanation:
In air medium the force is $F = \frac{{{q_1}{q_2}}}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}{r^2}}}$
In a medium of dielectric constant $k$, the force is
$F' = \frac{{{q_1}{q_2}}}{{4\pi k\varepsilon {R^2}}}$
As,$F' = 2F \Rightarrow \frac{{{q_1}{q_2}}}{{4\pi k{\varepsilon _0}{R^2}}} = \frac{{{q_1}{q_2}}}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}{r^2}}}$
$\therefore R = \frac{r}{{\sqrt {2k} }}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Force between two charges

question: Two small spheres each of mass $m$ and with a charge $q$ lie inside a nonconducting smooth hemispherical bowl of radius $R$. Find $q$ if the equilibrium separation between the two charges is $d$.
explanation:
The two charges are shown in the figure.
By considering the equilibrium of any charge
$N\cos \theta = \frac{{{q^2}}}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}{d^2}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
$N\sin \theta = mg\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
From both the equations
${\mathop{\rm Tan}\nolimits} \theta = \frac{{mg4\pi {\varepsilon _0}{d^2}}}{{{q^2}}} = \frac{{\sqrt {{R^2} - \frac{{{d^2}}}{4}} }}{{d/2}}$                                                                                                                                                                                                                                              $q = \sqrt {\frac{{2mg\pi {\varepsilon _0}{d^3}}}{{\sqrt {{R^2} - \frac{{{d^2}}}{4}} }}} $
subject: Physics
subtopic: Equilibrium of system of charges

question: Two charges ${Q_1}$ and ${Q_2}$ are present as shown.Two dielectrics of thickness ${t_1}$ and ${t_2}$ and dielectric constants ${k_1}$ and ${k_2}$ are introduced as shown. Find the force between the charges
explanation:
Effective distance in vacuum
$ = \sqrt {{k_1}} {t_1} + \sqrt {{k_2}} {t_2} + d - ({t_1} + {t_2})$
$F = \frac{{{Q_1}{Q_2}}}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}{{\left[ {\sqrt {{k_1}} {t_1} + \sqrt {{k_2}} {t_2} + d - ({t_1} + {t_2})} \right]}^2}}}$
subject: Physics

explanation: The resultant force acts as a restoring force and proportional to displacement. When it reaches the plane $XY$, the resultant force is zero and the mass moves down due to inertia. Thus oscillation is set.

question: Two charges $ + 4e$ and $ + e$ are at a distance  $x$ apart. At what distance, a charge  $q$ must be placed from charge $ + e$ so that it is in equilibrium
explanation:
For equilibrium of the charge $q$
$\frac{{q\left( e \right)}}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}{d^2}}} = \frac{{q\left( {4e} \right)}}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}{{\left( {x - d} \right)}^2}}}$
where $d$ is the distance of $q$ from the charge $e$.
$ \Rightarrow d = \frac{x}{3}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Equilibrium of system of charges

question: A charge $q$ is placed at the centre of the line  joining two equal charges $Q$. The system of three charges will be in equilibrium if $q$ is equal to
explanation:
The central charge is in the equilibrium. If the system has to be in equilibrium then the net force on any of the charge $Q$ should be zero.
$\frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{{QQ}}{{{l^2}}} + \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{{qQ}}{{{{\left( {\frac{l}{2}} \right)}^2}}} = 0$
$ \Rightarrow \,q = \frac{{ - Q}}{4}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Equilibrium of system of charges

question: Two point charges are 3 $m$ apart and their combined charge is $8\,\mu C$.The force of repulsion between them is $0.012\,N$, Charges  are
explanation:
Let ${q_1} = q\,\mu {\rm{C}}$ and ${q_2} = \left( {8 - q} \right)\mu C$
$r = 3\,m,{\rm{ }}F{\rm{ }} = {\rm{ }}0.012{\rm{ }}N$
According to Coulomb’s law
$F = \frac{{{q_1}{{\rm{q}}_2}}}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}{r^2}}}$
$0.012 = \frac{{9 \times {{10}^9}q\left( {8 - q} \right) \times {{10}^{ - 12}}}}{{{3^2}}}$
$0.012 = q\left( {8 - q} \right){10^{ - 3}}$
or ${q^2} - 8q + 12 = 0$
Solving the quadratic equation, we get
$q = 6$ or 2
Hence, the two charges are $6\,\mu C,{\rm{ }}2\,\mu C$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Force between two charges

question: Two spheres of electric charges $ + 2\,nC$ and $ - 8\,nC$ are placed at a distance $d$ apart. If they are  allowed to touch each other, what is the new  distance between them to get a repulsive force  of same magnitude as before?
explanation:
Force on each sphere
$ = \frac{{k{q_1}{q_1}}}{2} = \frac{{ - 16k}}{{{d^2}}} \times {10^{ - 18}}N$
When both the spheres are allowed to touch each other, the charges get redistributed.
Charge on each sphere $ = \frac{{(2 - 8)}}{2}nC = - 3nC$
Force on each sphere
$ = \frac{{k{{( - 3nC)}^2}}}{{{r^2}}} = \frac{{9k}}{{{r^2}}} \times {10^{ - 18}}N$
According to question,
$\frac{{16k}}{{{d^2}}} \times {10^{ - 18}} = \frac{{9k}}{{{r^2}}} \times {10^{ - 18}};r = \frac{3}{4}d$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Force between two charges

question: Two particles of equal mass $m$ and charge $q$ are placed at a distance of 16 $cm$. They do not experience any force. The value of $\frac{q}{m}$ is
explanation:
They will not experience any force if gravitational and electrostatic force are equal. $\left| {\overrightarrow {{F_g}} } \right| = \left| {\overrightarrow {{F_e}} } \right|$
$ \Rightarrow G\frac{{{m^2}}}{{{{(16 \times {{10}^{ - 2}})}^2}}} = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}} \cdot \frac{{{q^2}}}{{{{(16 \times {{10}^{ - 2}})}^2}}}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{q}{m} = \sqrt {4\pi {\varepsilon _0}G} $
subject: Physics
subtopic: Equilibrium of system of charges

question: Two copper balls, each weighing 10 $g$ are kept in air 10 $cm$ apart. If one electron from every ${10^6}$ atoms is transferred from one ball to the  other, the coulomb force between them is  (atomic weight of copper is $63.5g$)
explanation:
Number of atoms in given mass
$ = \frac{{10}}{{63.5}} \times 6.02 \times {10^{23}} = 9.48 \times {10^{22}}$
Transfer of electrons between balls
$ = \frac{{9.48 \times {{10}^{22}}}}{{{{10}^6}}}$
$ = 9.48 \times {10^{16}}$
Hence magnitude of charge gained by each ball.
$q = 9.48 \times {10^{16}} \times 1.6 \times {10^{ - 19}} = 0.015\,C$
Force of attraction between the balls
$F = 9 \times {10^9} \times \frac{{{{\left( {0.015} \right)}^2}}}{{{{\left( {0.1} \right)}^2}}} = 2 \times {10^8}N.$
subject: Physics

question: Two identical charges repel each other with a force equal to $10\,g\,wt$ when they are $0.9\,m$ apart in air.$(g = 10m{s^2})$.The value of each  charge is
explanation:
$F = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{{{q^2}}}{{{d^2}}}$
$\therefore (10 \times {10^{ - 3}}) \times 10 = \frac{{(9 \times {{10}^9}) \times {q^2}}}{{{{(0.9)}^2}}}$
$ \Rightarrow {q^2} = 9 \times {10^{ - 12}}$
$ \Rightarrow q = 3 \times {10^{ - 6}}C = 3\mu C$

question: Two point charges placed at a distance $r$ in air experience a certain force. Then the distance at which they will experience the same force in a medium of dielectric constant $K$ is
explanation:
As the forces are equal
$\frac{{{q_1}{q_2}}}{{{r^2}}} = \frac{{{q_1}{q_2}}}{{K{d^2}}}$ or $K\,{d^2} = {r^2}$ or $d = \frac{r}{{\sqrt K }}$

question: The force of repulsion between two identical positive charges when kept with a separation $r$ in air is $F$. Half the gap between the two charges is filled by a dielectic slab of dielectric constant $ = 4$.Then the new force of repulsion between those two charges becomes
explanation:
Let $q$ be each charge,
According to Coloumb's law, the force of repulsion between the two charges when kept in air is
$F = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{{{q^2}}}{{{r^2}}}$
When half of the gap between the two charges is filled with the dielectric slab of dielectric constant $(K = 4)$,the new force of repulsion between them becomes
$F' = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{{{q^2}}}{{((r - t) + t\sqrt {K{)^2}} }}$
(where $t$ is the thickness of the slab)
$ = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{{{q^2}}}{{{{\left[ {\left( {r - \frac{r}{2}} \right) + \frac{r}{2}\sqrt 4 } \right]}^2}}}$
$\left( {as\,t = \frac{r}{2},K = 4} \right)$
$ = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{{{q^2}}}{{{{\left( {\frac{r}{2} + r} \right)}^2}}} = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{{{q^2}}}{{{{\left( {\frac{3}{2}r} \right)}^2}}}$
$ = \frac{4}{9}\left( {\frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{{{q^2}}}{{{r^2}}}} \right) = \frac{{4F}}{9}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Force between two charges

question: Two spheres carrying charges $ + 6\,\mu C$ and $ + 9\,\mu C$, separated by a distance $d$, experience a force of repulsion $F$. When a charge of $ - 3\,\mu C$ is given to both the spheres and kept at the same distance as before, the new force of repulsion is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764949748/preptracker/questions/q_30491_explanation_1764949747823.png Let us call spheres as $A$ and $B$.
Accroding to Coulomb's law, the force of repulsion between $A$ and $B$ separated by a distance $d$ is
$F = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{{( + 6\mu C)( + 9\mu C)}}{{{d^2}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\rm{(1)}}$
When a charge of $ - 3\,\mu C$ is given to both the spheres, then charge on $A =  + 6\,\mu C - 3\,\mu C =  + 3\,\mu C$ and on $B =  + 9\,\mu C - 3\,\mu C = 6\,\mu C$
Again by Columbo's law, then new force of repulsion between $A$ and $B$ separated by the same distance is
$F' = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{{{\rm{(}} + 3\mu C{\rm{)(}} + 6\mu C{\rm{)}}}}{{{d^2}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\rm{(2)}}$
Dividing equ (2) eqn (1), we get
$\frac{{F'}}{F} = \frac{{\frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{{{\rm{(}} + 3\mu C{\rm{)(}} + 6\mu C{\rm{)}}}}{{{d^2}}}}}{{\frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{{{\rm{(}} + 6\mu C{\rm{)(}} + 9\mu C{\rm{)}}}}{{{d^2}}}}}$
$ = \frac{{{\rm{(}} + 3\mu C{\rm{)(}} + 6\mu C{\rm{)}}}}{{{\rm{(}} + 6\mu C{\rm{)(}} + 9\mu C{\rm{)}}}} = \frac{1}{3}$ or $F' = \frac{F}{3}$
subject: Physics

question: Two equal point charges each of $3\mu C$ are separated by a certain distance in metres. If they are located at $(\hat i + \hat j + \hat k)$ and $(2\hat i + 3\hat j + 3\hat k),$ the electrostatic force between them is
explanation:
Here, ${q_1} = 3 \times {10^{ - 6}}C,\,{q_2} = 3 \times {10^{ - 6}}C$
${r_1} = \hat i + \hat j + \hat k,{r_2} = 2\hat i + 3\hat j + 3\hat k$
$\therefore \overrightarrow r = \overrightarrow {{r_2}} - \overrightarrow {{r_1}} $
$ = (2\hat i + 3\hat j + 3\hat k) - (\hat i + \hat j + \hat k) = \hat i + 2\hat j + 2\hat k$
The distance between the two charges is
$\left| {\overrightarrow {{r_1}} } \right| = \sqrt {{{(1)}^2} + {{(2)}^2} + {{(2)}^2}} = 3$
According to coulomb’s law,
$F = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}} \cdot \frac{{{q_1}{q_2}}}{{{r^2}}}$
$ = \frac{{9 \times {{10}^9} \times 3 \times {{10}^{ - 6}} \times 3 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}}}{{{{(3)}^2}}} = 9 \times {10^{ - 3}}N$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Force between two charges

question: A certain charge 2$Q$ is divided at first into two parts ${q_1}$ and ${q_2}$. Later the charges are placed at a certain distance. If the force of interaction between two charges is maximum then $\frac{Q}{{{q_1}}} = $ ……
explanation:
Let $r$ be the distance between ${q_1}$ and ${q_2}$. According to Coulomb's law, the force between them is
$F = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{{{q_1}{q_2}}}{{{r^2}}}$$ = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{{{q_1}(2Q - {q_1})}}{{{r^2}}}$
$(\because \,\,{q_1} + {q_2} = 2Q)$
For $F$ to be maximum $\frac{{dF}}{{d{q_1}}} = 0$ as $Q$ and $r$ are constant,
$\frac{d}{{d{q_1}}}\left[ {{q_1}(2Q - {q_1})} \right] = 0$
$2Q - 2{q_1} = 0;$ Hence,$\frac{Q}{{{q_1}}} = 1$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Force between two charges

question: The nucleus of helium atom contains two protons that are separated by distance $3.0 \times {10^{ - 15}}m$. The  magnitude of the electrostatic force that each proton exerts on the other is
explanation:
Charge of proton, ${q_p} = 1.6 \times {10^{ - 19}}\,C$
Distance between the protons, $r = 3 \times {10^{ - 15}}\,m$
The magnitude of electrostatic force between then is
${{\rm{F}}_e} = \frac{{{q_p}{q_p}}}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}{r^2}}} = \frac{{9 \times {{10}^9} \times 1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 19}} \times 1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 19}}}}{{{{\left( {3 \times {{10}^{ - 15}}} \right)}^2}}}$
$ = 25.6\,N$
subject: Physics

question: Force of attraction between two point electric charges placed at a distance $d$ in a medium is $F$.   What distance apart should these be kept in the  same medium, so that force between them  becomes $F/3$?
explanation:
Let ${q_1}$ and ${q_2}$ be the two point charges. The force between the charges, at a separation of $d$.
$F = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}} \cdot \frac{{{q_1}\,{q_2}}}{{{d^2}}}$
Suppose that force between the two charges become $F/3$, when the charges are kept at a distance apart. Then
$\frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}} \cdot \frac{{{q_1}\,{q_2}}}{{{x^2}}} = \frac{F}{3}$
or $\frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}} \cdot \frac{{{q_1}\,{q_2}}}{{{x^2}}} = \frac{1}{3}\left( {\frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}} \cdot \frac{{{q_1}\,{q_2}}}{{{d^2}}}} \right)$
or $x = \sqrt 3 \,d$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Force between two charges

question: An electron moving around the nucleus of a hydrogen atom in a circular orbit of radius $r$.  The coulomb force  $\overrightarrow F $ between the two is (where $K = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}$)
explanation:

$\vec F = - k\frac{{{e^2}}}{{{r^2}}}\hat r = - k \cdot \frac{{{e^2}}}{{{r^3}}}\vec r\left[ {\because \hat r = \frac{{\vec r}}{r}} \right]$

question: Two positively charged spheres of masses, ${m_1}\,{\rm{and}}\,{m_2}$ are suspended from a common point at the ceiling by identical insulating massless strings of length $l$. Charges on the two spheres are ${q_1}\,{\rm{and}}\,{q_2}$,respectively. At equilibrium both strings make the same angle $\theta $ with the vertical.Then
explanation:
The forces on the two masses are shown in the figure. Let $\theta $ is the angle made by the strings with vertical https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764949738/preptracker/questions/q_30317_explanation_1764949737918.png For equilibrium of ${m_1}$
${T_1}\cos \theta = {m_1}g$
${T_1}\sin \theta = F$
$\tan \theta = \frac{F}{{{m_1}g}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
For equilibrium of ${m_2}$
${T_2}\cos \theta = {m_2}g$
${T_2}\sin \theta = F$
$\tan \theta = \frac{F}{{{m_1}g}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
From (1) & (2)
${m_1} = {m_2}$

question:

Volume charge density is defined by the charge per unit length of a wire.

Charges add up algebraically like real numbers because they are scalars.
Mass and charge are similar in that both are always conserved quantities.
explanation: Statement A is true because charges are indeed scalars and add up algebraically. Statement B is false because mass is always conserved but charge is conserved only in an isolated system and not under all circumstances. Correct option is (2)

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.

Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct option given below:
If an isolated system consists of many charged bodies, the total charge of the system may change due to interactions among the bodies.
The total electric charge in an isolated system is conserved, even though charge carriers like electrons or protons may be created or destroyed.
explanation: Statement A is false because even though charges may get redistributed among bodies in an isolated system, the total charge of the system remains conserved. Statement B is true as it correctly states the law of conservation of charge, which asserts that the total charge in an isolated system is always conserved, even if the charge carriers are created or destroyed. Correct option is (3)

Charge conservation implies that in any closed system, the total charge remains constant over time.

question:
Read the Assertion and Reason carefully to mark correct option given below:

Assertion: Surface charge density represents a smoothed out average of the microscopic charge density over a macroscopic area.

Reason: Surface charge density is defined in terms of discrete charges at the microscopic level.
explanation: The assertion is true as surface charge density ( $\sigma$ ) is indeed a macroscopic average of the charge distribution, ignoring the discrete nature of charge at a microscopic level. The reason is true in the sense that the concept ofsurface charge density arises because it is impractical to work with discrete charges for macroscopic applications. However, it is not the correct explanation for the assertion. Surface charge density is a concept used to simplify the description of charge distribution at a scale where it is impractical to account for individual charges. Correct option is (2)

question: A simple pendulum consists of a small sphere of mass $m$ suspended by a thread of length $l$. The sphere carries a positive charge $q$. The pendulum is allowed to do small oscillations in uniform electric field $E$ with direction vertically upwards. The time period of oscillation is
explanation:
The given situation is shown below. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952814/preptracker/questions/q_111862_explanation_1764952814404.png Effective gravitational acceleration $g_{\text {eff }}=\left(g-\dfrac{q E}{m}\right)$
$\therefore \quad T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g_{\text {eff }}}}=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{g-\dfrac{q E}{m}}}$

explanation:

question:
Assertion: If the bob of a simple pendulum is kept in a horizontal electric field, its period of oscillation will remain same.

Reason: If bob is charged and kept in horizontal electric field, then the time period will be decreased.
explanation: When an uncharged bob of a pendulum is placed in an electric field, it does not affect its time period as no electric force can be experienced by the bob since it is neutral. So, the acceleration of the bob remains same, i.e., $g$ and hence its time period. But when a charged bob of a pendulum is placed in horizontal electric field, the bob experiences an electric force in horizontal direction due to which the effective value of increases. Hence its time period decreases.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.

question:
Assertion: The lightning conductor at the top of high building has sharp pointed ends.

Reason: The surface density of charge at sharp points is very high resulting in setting up of electric wind.
explanation:
The lightning conductor at the top of a high building has sharp pointed ends. This is correct because lightning conductors often have pointed ends to facilitate the discharge of electric charges.
Reason explains why the sharp pointed ends are used. It correctly states that the surface density of charge at sharp points is very high, which results in the setting up of an electric wind. This electric wind helps in the discharge of electric charges to nearby charged clouds and reduces the chances of lightning striking the building. If lightning does strike, the charge is conducted to the earth, protecting the building.
The Reason provides a valid scientific explanation for the Assertion, so the correct choice is (1).

explanation:

question: Match the terms related to electric dipoles in external fields (uniform or non-uniform) in Column I with their corresponding descriptions in Column II and choose the correct options from the codes given below.
explanation:
$\mathrm{A}-\mathrm{Q}$ : A permanent dipole has a dipole moment ($p$) that exists irrespective of an external field $E$ ; it is not induced by the field .
$\mathrm{B}-\mathrm{R}$ : A torque is exerted on a dipole in a uniform external field, which tends to align the dipole with the direction of the field; when the dipole is aligned with the field, the torque is zero .
C - S : The net force on a dipole in a uniform external field is zero because the forces on the positive and negative charges cancel each other due to the uniformity of the field .
D - P: In a non-uniform field, a dipole experiences a net force, and the direction and magnitude of this force depends on the orientation of the dipole's moment ( $p$ ) with respect to the field $(E).$ Correct option is (4)

options.text: A - R,        	B - P,	C - Q ,  	D - S
A - Q , 	B - S, 	C - P,	D - R
A - P, 	B - Q , 	C - R,	D - S
A - Q ,  	B - R, 	C - S, 	D - P

question: Match the concepts related to electric dipoles in Column I with their corresponding descriptions in Column II and choose the correct options from the codes given below.
explanation:
$\mathrm{A}-\mathrm{Q}$ : By convention, the direction of an electric dipole is from the negative charge $(-q)$ to the positive charge ($q$), defining a specific orientation in space .
B - R: The mid-point between the two charges of a dipole, which are separated by a distance $2 a$, is called the centre of the dipole .
C - S : The total charge of an electric dipole is zero because it consists of a pair of equal and opposite point charges ($q$ and $-q$).
D - P: The electric field due to a dipole at distances much larger than the separation between the charges falls off faster than the $1 / r^{2}$ dependence that characterizes the field due to a single charge. Correct option is (4)

explanation: Statement  A  is false because a dipole aligns itself with a uniform external electric field due to torque, not a net force; the net force on the dipole in a uniform field is zero. Statement B is true because the torque on a dipole in a uniform external electric field is maximum when the dipole is perpendicular to the field. Correct option is (3)

Reason: The forces acting on the charges of the dipole are equal and opposite, resulting in a net force of zero.
explanation: The assertion is true as a dipole in a uniform external field does experience a torque, as the forces acting at different points on the dipole create a couple. The reason is true and is the correct explanation for the assertion because the net force on the dipole is indeed zero due to the uniform field, and it's the separation of the charges that causes a torque which tends to align the dipole with the field. question: Three charges are arranged on the vertices of a right angle triangle as shown in the figure. The magnitude of dipole moment of the combination in the unit of ${C}-{cm}$ is
explanation:
The given arrangement of system of charges are shown as https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957631/preptracker/questions/q_111855_explanation_1764957630591.png In $\triangle A B C$,
$\sin 30^{\circ}=\dfrac{B C}{A C} \Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{2}=\dfrac{B C}{10} \Rightarrow B C=5 {~cm}$
Similarly,
$A B=A C \sin 60^{\circ}=10 \times \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}=5 \sqrt{3} {~cm}$
The given charges form two electric dipole of dipole moment $p_{1}$ and $p_{2}$ as shown is figure https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957631/preptracker/questions/q_111855_explanation_1764957631270.png Since, $p_{1}=A B \times q=5 \sqrt{3} q(C-m)$
and $p_{2}=B C \times q=5 q C-m$
The magnitude of dipole moment of the combination
$p=\sqrt{p_{1}^{2}+p_{2}^{2}}=\sqrt{(5 \sqrt{3} q)^{2}+(5 q)^{2}}$
$\Rightarrow p=\sqrt{100 q^{2}}=10 q$

question: A neutral water molecule $\left( {{H_2}O} \right)$ in its vapour state has an electric dipole moment of magnitude $6.4 \times {10^{ - 30}}C - m$. How far apart are the molecules centres of positive and negative charges?
explanation:
There are 10 electrons and 10 protons in a neutral water molecule.
So, its dipole moment is $p=q(2 l)=10 e(2 l)$
Hence length of the dipole $i.e.,$ distance between centres of positive and negative charges is
$2l = \frac{p}{{10e}} = \frac{{6.4 \times {{10}^{ - 30}}}}{{10 \times 1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 19}}}}$
$ = 4 \times {10^{ - 12}}\,m = 4\,pm$

$ = 6.88 \times {10^{ - 28}}C - m$

question: A dipole comprises of two charged particles of identical magnitude $q$ and opposite in nature. The mass ' $m$ ' of the positive charged particle is half of the mass of the negative charged particle. The two charges are separated by a distance ' $l$ ' the dipole is pivoted at the mid point of dipole axis. If the dipole is placed in a uniform electric field $\vec{E}$; in such a way that dipole axis makes a very small angle with the electric field $\vec{E}$. The angular frequency of the oscillations of the dipole when released is given by
explanation:
As, $\tau = pE\sin \theta \Rightarrow \tau \approx pE\theta $ (for small angle $\sin \theta \approx \theta $) $I\alpha = pE\theta \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} (\because \tau = I\alpha )$
So, $\alpha = \frac{{pE}}{I}\theta $
$ \Rightarrow {\omega ^2} = \frac{{pE}}{I} \Rightarrow \omega = \sqrt {\frac{{pE}}{I}} {\text{ }}\left( {\because \alpha = {\omega ^2}\theta } \right)\quad \quad (1)$
$\therefore \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,M.I.,\,\,\,I = \frac{{2m{l^2}}}{4} + \frac{{m{l^2}}}{4} = \frac{{3m{l^2}}}{4}\quad $
(put in (1))
$\omega = \sqrt {\frac{{q(l)E}}{{3m\left( {\frac{{{l^2}}}{4}} \right)}}} = \sqrt {\frac{{4qE}}{{3ml}}} $ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764949875/preptracker/questions/q_31669_explanation_1764949875446.png

explanation:

$\theta=90^{\circ}$
$E=\dfrac{K p}{r^{3}} \Rightarrow E \propto \dfrac{1}{r^{3}}$

question: Two charges each of magnitude $0.01 C$ and separated by a distance of $0.4\,mm$ constitute an electric dipole.If the dipole is placed in an uniform electric field '$\vec{E}$' of $10{\rm{ }}dyne/C$ making $30^{\circ}$ angle with $\vec{E}$, the magnitude of torque acting on dipole is
explanation:
Given, $q = 0.01C,l = 0.4\,mm = 0.4 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,m$
Dipole moment, $p=q(l)$
$ = 0.01 \times 0.4 \times {10^{ - 3}} = 4 \times {10^{ - 6}}C - m$
$E = 10\,\,dyne/C = 10 \times {10^{ - 5}}\,N/C$
$ = {10^{ - 4}}\,N/C$
Torque acting on the dipole is
$\tau=p E \sin \theta$,
where $\theta$ is the angle between $\vec{P}$ and $\vec{E}$.$=4 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{-4} \times \sin 30^{\circ}$
$=4 \times 10^{-10} \times \dfrac{1}{2}$
$=2 \times 10^{-10} N-m$

question: In the situation shown in the diagram, magnitude of $q <  < |Q|$ and $r>>a$. The net force on the free charge $-q$ and net torque on it about $O$ at the instant shown are respectively $[p = 2aQ$ is the dipole moment]
explanation:
Electric field due to dipole at point $P$ is
$E=\dfrac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \dfrac{p}{r^{3}}$ (opposite to the direction of dipole)
$\overline F = - q \cdot \overline E $
$ \Rightarrow \overline F = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{{qp}}{{{r^3}}}\hat i$
Torque $\vec{\tau}=\vec{r} \times \vec{F}$
$\Rightarrow \vec{\tau}=-\dfrac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \dfrac{p q}{r^{2}} \hat{k}$.
Correct option is (3).

question: An electric dipole is placed at an angle of $30^{\circ}$ with an electric field of intensity $2 \times {10^5}N{C^{ - 1}}$. It experiences a torque equal to $4Nm$. Calculate the magnitude of charge on the dipole, if the dipole length is $2\,cm$.
explanation:
$\tau$ on a dipole $=\vec{p} \times \vec{E}$
$\Rightarrow \tau=p E \sin \theta$
$\Rightarrow 4=q \times l \times E \times \sin 30^{\circ}$
$\Rightarrow 4=q \times 2 \times 10^{-2} \times 2 \times 10^{5} \times \dfrac{1}{2}$
$ \Rightarrow q = 2 \times {10^{ - 3}} \Rightarrow q = 2\,\,mC$
Correct option is (3).

question: Two charges $ + 10\mu C\,\,\,{\mathop{\rm and}\nolimits} \,\,\, - 10\mu C$ are placed  20 $mm$ apart. The electric field at point $P$, on the  axis of the dipole 100 $cm$ away from its centre  $O$ on the side of the positive charge is
explanation:

$d = 20\,\,mm = 20 \times {10^{ - 3}}m$
$r = OP = 100\,\,cm = 1m$
$\left| {\vec p} \right| = q \times d = 10 \times {10^{ - 6}} \times 20 \times {10^{ - 3}} = 2 \times {10^{ - 7}}\,Cm$
The electric field along $BP$,
As $d < < r,$
$\vec E = \frac{{2\left| {\vec p} \right|}}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}{r^3}}} = \frac{{2 \times 2 \times {{10}^{ - 7}} \times 9 \times {{10}^9}}}{{{{\left( 1 \right)}^3}}}$

question: An electric dipole is placed along the $x$-axis at  the origin $O$. A point $P$ is at a distance of 10 $cm$ from this origin such that $OP$ makes an angle $\frac{\pi }{6}$ with $x$-axis. If the electric field at $P$ makes  an angle $\beta $ with the $x$-axis, the value of $\beta $ would be
explanation:
Let ${E_1}\,{\rm{and}}\,{E_2}$ be the fields along and perpendicular to the radial line $OP$ respectively
From figure, $\beta = \frac{\pi }{6} + \alpha ,$ where
$\tan \alpha = \frac{{{E_2}}}{{{E_1}}} = \left( {\frac{{kp\,\sin \theta }}{{{r^3}}}} \right)\left( {\frac{{{r^3}}}{{2kp\cos \theta }}} \right) = \frac{1}{2}\tan \theta $
$\tan \left( {\beta - \frac{\pi }{6}} \right) = \frac{1}{2}\tan \frac{\pi }{6} = \frac{1}{{2\sqrt 3 }}$
$\beta - \frac{\pi }{6} = {\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{1}{{2\sqrt 3 }}} \right)$
$\beta = \frac{\pi }{6} + {\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{1}{{2\sqrt 3 }}} \right)$

question: Three small dipoles are arranged as shown below. What will be the net electric field at point $O\left( {k = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}} \right)$
explanation:
The fields produced by the three dipoles are
${{\vec E}_1} = \frac{{ - kp}}{{{x^3}}}\hat j,{{\vec E}_2} = \frac{{ - k\left( {2p} \right)}}{{{x^3}}}\hat i,{{\vec E}_3} = \frac{{ - k\left( {3p} \right)}}{{{x^3}}}\hat j$
${{\vec E}_{net}} = {{\vec E}_1} + {{\vec E}_2} + {{\vec E}_3} = \frac{{ - 2kp}}{{{x^3}}}\hat i - \frac{{4kp}}{{{x^3}}}\hat j$
${{\vec E}_{net}} = 2\sqrt 5 \frac{{kp}}{{{x^3}}}$

question: The electric intensity due to a dipole of length  1$m$ and having charge of $100\,\mu C$ at a point  on the axial line at a distance 2$m$ from one of the  charges in air, is
explanation:
Given that Length of the dipole $(d) = 1\,m$
Charge on the dipole
$\left( q \right) = 100\,\mu C = {10^{ - 4}}C$ and distance of the point on the axis from the mid-point of the dipole $\left( r \right) = 2 + 0.5 = 2.5\,\,m.$
We know that the electric field intensity due to dipole on the given point ($E$)
As $r$ and $d$ are comparable to each other.
$ = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}} \times \frac{{2\left( {q.d} \right)r}}{{{{\left( {{r^2} - \left( {\frac{d}{2}} \right)} \right)}^2}}}$
$ = 9 \times {10^9} \times \frac{{2\left( {{{10}^{ - 4}} \times 1} \right) \times 2.5}}{{{{\left[ {{{\left( {2.5} \right)}^2} - {{\left( {0.5} \right)}^2}} \right]}^2}}}$
$ = \frac{{18 \times 2.5 \times {{10}^5}}}{{36}} = 1.25 \times {10^5}\,\,N{\rm{/}}C$ ($k = 1$ for air)

question: A short electric dipole of dipole moment ${\vec p}$ is placed at a distance $r$ from the centre of a solid metallic sphere of radius $a\left( { < < r} \right)$ as shown in the figure. The electric field intensity at the centre of sphere $C$ due to induced charge on the sphere is
explanation:
Electric field at $C$ due to electric dipole
$ = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{{2p}}{{{r^3}}}\,\,{\rm{along}}\,\,OC$ $\left( {{\rm{since}}\,\,r > > a} \right)$
We know that net electric field at $C$ is zero. So electric field at $C$ due to induced charges must be equal and opposite to electric field due to dipole.
i.e., ${{\vec E}_{Dipole}} + {{\vec E}_{induced\,\,ch\arg e}} = 0\left( {{\rm{at}}\,\,{\rm{point}}\,\,C} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow {{\vec E}_{induced\,\,ch\arg e}} = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{{2p}}{{{r^3}}}\left( {{\rm{along}}\,\,CO} \right)$

question: Three small dipoles are arranged as shown below. What will be the net electric field at  $O\left( {k = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}} \right)$
explanation:
Point $O$ lies at axial positions of dipole 1 and 2 and equatorial position of dipole 3.
Hence field at $O$ is
${E_1} = \frac{{k2p}}{{{x^3}}}$ (towards right) due to dipole 1
${E_2} = \frac{{k2p}}{{{x^3}}}$ (towards right) due to dipole 2
${E_3} = \frac{{k\left( {4p} \right)}}{{{x^3}}}$ (towards left) due to dipole 3
The net field at $P$ is zero.

question: Electric charges $2q,2q, - 4q$ are placed at the corners of an equilateral $\Delta \,ABC$ of side $l$. The magnitude of electric dipole moment of the system is
explanation:
The dipole moments of the combination is shown in the following figure
The charge $ - 4q$ can be considered as a combination of two charges each of charge $ - 2q$. So $ + 2q$ forms dipole with $ - 2q$ . So we have two dipoles.
Net dipole moment, i.e.,
${p_{net}} = \sqrt {{p^2} + {p^2} + 2pp\,\,\cos \,\,60^\circ } = \sqrt 3 p$
$ = \sqrt 3 \left( {2ql} \right) = 2\sqrt 3 ql\left( {p = ql} \right)$
subject: Physics

question: Two electric dipoles of moment $p$ and 27$p$ are  placed in opposite direction on a line at a distance  of 10 $cm$. The electric field will be zero at point  between the dipoles whose distance from the  dipole of moment $p$ is
explanation:
Suppose neutral point $N$ lies at a distance $x$ from dipole of moment $p$ or at a distance
$(10 - x)$ from dipole of 27 $p$
At $N,{E_1} = {E_2}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}} \cdot \frac{{2p}}{{{x^3}}} = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}} \cdot \frac{{2\left( {27\,p} \right)}}{{{{\left( {10 - x} \right)}^3}}}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{1}{{{x^3}}} = \frac{{27}}{{{{\left( {10 - x} \right)}^3}}} \Rightarrow x = 2.5\,\,cm$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Field produced by multiple dipoles and dipole moment of two or more charges

question: An electric dipole is placed at an angle of $60^\circ $  with an electric field of intensity $\sqrt 3  \times {10^5}\,N{C^{ - 1}}$.  It experiences a torque equal to 6 $N m$. The  charge on the dipole if the dipole length is 4 $cm$  is
explanation:
Here,$E = \sqrt 3 \times {10^5}N{C^{ - 1}},\,l = 4\,cm,\,\tau = 6\,Nm$
Torque, $\vec \tau = \vec p \times \vec E;\tau = pE\,\,\sin \theta $
$\therefore 6 = p \times \sqrt 3 \times {10^5} \times \sin 60^\circ $ or $p = 4 \times {10^{ - 5}}Cm$
$\therefore \,\,\,{\rm{Charge}},\,\,q = \frac{p}{l} = \frac{{4 \times {{10}^{ - 5}}Cm}}{{0.04\,\,m}}$
$ = 1 \times {10^{ - 3}}C = 1\,\,mC$

subtopic: Dipole in a Uniform External electrostatic Field

question: An electric dipole is situated in an electric field  of uniform intensity $E$ whose dipole moment is  $p$ and moment of inertia is $I$. If the dipole is  displaced slightly from the equilibrium position,  then the time period of its oscillation is
explanation:
As, torque i.e., $\tau = pE\,\,\sin \,\theta = I\alpha $
For small $\theta ,\alpha = \frac{{ - pE}}{I}\theta $(As $\alpha $ and $\theta $are opposite)
$\omega = \sqrt {\frac{{pE}}{I}} $
$T = \frac{{2\pi }}{\omega } = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{I}{{pE}}} $

$ = 0.045\,\,N{C^{ - 1}}$

question: When an electric dipole $p$ is placed in a uniform electric field $E$, then at what angle between $p$ and $E$ the value of torque will be minimum?
explanation:

$\left| \tau \right| = pE\,\,\sin \,\,\theta = 0$
$\therefore $ Torque is minimum, when
$\theta = 0^\circ \,\,or\,\,180^\circ $

question: Two charges $ \pm 20\,\mu C$ are placed 10 $mm$ apart. The electric field at point $P$, On the axis of the dipole 10 $cm$ away from its centre $O$ on the side of the positive charge is
explanation:
Here,$q = \pm 20\mu C = \pm 20 \times {10^{ - 6}}\,C$
$2a = 10\,mm = 10 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,m$
$r = OP = 10\,cm = 10 \times {10^{ - 2}}\,m$
$\left| {\overrightarrow p } \right| = q \times 2a = 20 \times {10^{ - 6}} \times 10 \times {10^{ - 3}} = 2 \times {10^{ - 7}}\,m$
The electric field along $BP$,
$E = \frac{{2pr}}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}{{\left( {{r^2} - {a^2}} \right)}^2}}}$
As $a < < r,$
$ = 3.6 \times {10^6}\,N\,{C^{ - 1}}$

question: An electric dipole is formed by two particles  fixed at the ends of a light rigid rod of length $l$.   The mass of each particle is $m$ and charges are  $ - q\,{\rm{and}}\, + q$. The system is suspended by a  torsionless thread in an electric field of intensity  $E$ such that the dipole axis is parallel to the field.  If it is slightly displaced,  the period of angular  motion is
explanation:
The torque on the dipole is
$\tau = pE\,\,\sin \theta $
$\tau = I\alpha $
$I\alpha = pE\,\theta ({\rm{For}}\,{\rm{small}}\,\theta )$
The moment of inertia is
$I = 2\left( {m{{\left( {\frac{l}{2}} \right)}^2}} \right) = \frac{{m{l^2}}}{2}$
$\therefore $ Time period $ = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{I}{{pE}}} = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{{ml}}{{2qE}}} $

question: An electric dipole is placed at an angle of $30^\circ $  with an electric field of intensity  $4 \times {10^5}\,N{C^{ - 1}}$ , It experiences a torque of $8\,N - m$.  The charge on the dipole, if the dipole  length is 4 $cm$ will be
explanation:
Torque,$\vec \tau = \vec p \times \vec E = pE\,\sin \,\theta $
$8 = p \times 4 \times {10^5} \times \sin \,\,30^\circ $
$ \Rightarrow p = 4 \times {10^{ - 5}}\,\,C{\rm{ - }}m$
Dipole moment, $p = q \times l$
$q = \frac{p}{l} = \frac{{4 \times {{10}^{ - 5}}}}{{0.04}} = 1 \times {10^{ - 3}}C = 1\,mC$

question: A tiny spherical oil drop carrying a net charge $q$ is balanced in still air, with a vertical uniform electric field of strength $\frac{{81}}{7}\pi  \times {10^5}\,V/m$. When the field is switched off, the drop is observed to fall with terminal velocity $2 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,m{s^{ - 1}}$. Here $g = 9.8\,m/{s^2}$, Viscosity of air is $1.8 \times {10^{ - 5}}Ns/{m^2}$ and the density of oil is $900\,kg\,{m^{ - 3}}$. Then magnitude of ' $q$ ' is
explanation:
Terminal velocity ${v_T} = \frac{{2{a^2}(\rho - \sigma )g}}{{9\eta }}$.
As density of oil $(\rho)$ is much higher than that of air $(\sigma)$,
${v_T} \approx \frac{{2{a^2}\rho \,g}}{{9\eta }} \Rightarrow a = \sqrt {\frac{{9\eta {v_T}}}{{2\rho \,g}}} .$
$\therefore$ Radius of oil drop,
$a = \sqrt {\frac{{9 \times 1.8 \times {{10}^{ - 5}} \times 2 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{2 \times 900 \times 9.8}}} = \frac{3}{7} \times {10^{ - 5}}\,m$.
When the oil drop is balanced in air,
$qE = mg = \frac{4}{3}\pi {a^3}\rho \,g \Rightarrow q = \frac{{4\pi {a^3}\rho \,g}}{{3E}}$
$ \Rightarrow q = \frac{{4\pi \times {{\left( {\frac{3}{7} \times {{10}^{ - 5}}} \right)}^3} \times 900 \times 9.8}}{{3 \times \frac{{81\pi }}{7} \times {{10}^5}}}$
$ = 8 \times {10^{ - 19}}C$
subject: Physics

question: Two point charges of $-8 \mu C$ and $+32 \mu C$ are separated by a distance of $15\,cm$ in air. The position of the point from $-8 \mu C$ charge at which the resultant electric field becomes zero is
explanation:
Let the distance of the point $R$ from $-8 \mu c$ at which resultant electric field becomes zero be $x$. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957640/preptracker/questions/q_111958_explanation_1764957640252.png At point $R, E_{R}=0$
$\therefore E_{P}=E_{Q}$
$\therefore \dfrac{8}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} x^{2}}=\dfrac{32}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}(15+x)^{2}}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{x+15}{x}=2$
On solving $x=15$

question: Two point charges $-10 \mu C$ and $+5 \mu C$ are situated on $X$-axis at $x=0$ and $x = \sqrt 2 \,m$. The point along the $X$-axis where the electric field becomes zero is
explanation:
$q_{1}=-10 \mu C, q_{2}=5 \mu C$
$r=\sqrt{2} m$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957643/preptracker/questions/q_111957_explanation_1764957643089.png Distance of the point on $X$-axis from charge $q_{2}$ where electric field is zero,
$d=\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{q_{2}}}{\sqrt{q_{1}}-\sqrt{q_{2}}}\right) r=\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{5}}{\sqrt{10}-\sqrt{5}}\right) \sqrt{2}$
$=\dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{2}-1} m$.
Distance of neutral point $P$ from the origin
$=\sqrt{2}+d=\sqrt{2}+\dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{2}-1}=2(\sqrt{2}+1) m$

question: A proton and an ' $\alpha$ ' particle are both accelerated from rest in a uniform electric field. The ratio of works done by the electric field on the proton and the -particle in a given time is
explanation:
Acceleration of charged particle in an electric field is
$a=\dfrac{q E}{m} \therefore v=u+a t=\left(\dfrac{q E}{m}\right) t \Rightarrow v \propto \dfrac{q}{m}$
$\therefore$ Work done by electric field,
$W=\Delta K=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2} \Rightarrow W \propto m v^{2} \propto m\left(\dfrac{q}{m}\right)^{2}$
$\therefore W \propto \dfrac{q^{2}}{m}$
$\therefore \dfrac{W_{p}}{W_{\alpha}}=\left(\dfrac{q_{p}}{q_{\alpha}}\right)^{2}\left(\dfrac{m_{\alpha}}{m_{p}}\right)=\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{2}\left(\dfrac{4}{1}\right)=1: 1$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Motion of charged particle/body in electrostatic field

question: Match the terms related to electric field lines representation in Column I with their corresponding meanings in Column II and choose the correct options from the codes given below.
explanation:
A - Q : The electric field $(E)$ due to a point charge is represented pictorially with vectors whose lengths are proportional to the strength of the field at each point, decreasing inversely with the square of the distance from the charge.
B - R: The density of the electric field lines represents the magnitude of the electric field; where the field is strong, the lines are closer, and where it is weak, the lines are further apart.
$\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{S}$ : The direction of the electric field lines from a positive charge is radially outward, as indicated by the arrows pointing away from the charge in the illustrative figure.
D - P: The number of electric field lines that can be drawn in any region is theoretically infinite; what matters is the relative density of these lines in different regions. Correct option is (4)

options.text: A - R,        	B - P, 	C - Q , 	D - S
A - R, 	B - Q ,   	C - S,	D - P
A - P, 	B - Q, 	C - R,	D - S
A - Q,  	B - R, 	C - S, 	D - P

question: Match the electric field properties in Column I with their corresponding characteristics in Column II and choose the correct options from the codes given below.
explanation:
A - Q : The electric field $(E)$ due to a positive charge is directed radially outwards from the charge .
B - R: The electric field due to a negative charge points radially inwards towards the charge .
C - S: Electrostatic field lines do not form closed loops; they start at positive charges and end at negative charges .
D - P: The electric field due to a point charge has spherical symmetry, with the magnitude of the field being the same on a sphere centered at the point charge. Correct option is (4)

question: Match the terms related to electric field in Column I with their appropriate descriptions in Column II and choose the correct options from the codes given below.
explanation:
A - Q : The electric field $(E)$ is a vector quantity that varies from one point to another in space, and its value at any point is determined by the positions of the source charges .
B - R: A source charge $(Q)$ is responsible for producing an electric field in the surrounding space.
C - S: A test charge $(q)$ is used to test the effect of the electric field and experiences a force $(F)$ when placed within the field .
D - P: The SI unit of the electric field is newton per Coulomb $(N / C)$, which defines the force experienced per unit charge in the field. Correct option is (4)

question:
Read the Assertion and Reason carefully to mark correct option given below:

Assertion: Surface charge density represents a smoothed out average of the microscopic charge density over a macroscopic area.

Reason: Surface charge density is defined in terms of discrete charges at the microscopic level.
explanation: The assertion is true as surface charge density ( $\sigma$ ) is indeed a macroscopic average of the charge distribution, ignoring the discrete nature of charge at a microscopic level. The reason is true in the sense that the concept ofsurface charge density arises because it is impractical to work with discrete charges for macroscopic applications. However, it is not the correct explanation for the assertion. Surface charge density is a concept used to simplify the description of charge distribution at a scale where it is impractical to account for individual charges. Correct option is (2)

subject: Physics
