question:
Read the Assertion and Reason carefully to mark correct option given below:

Assertion: The electric field intensity due to a point charge diminishes with the square of the distance from the charge.

Reason: Electric field lines are more closely spaced near the charge and spread out with distance.
explanation: The electric field intensity due to a point charge is given by $E=\dfrac{k Q}{r^{2}}$, where $r$ is the distance from the charge, which justifies the assertion. The reason is a correct explanation because the closeness of electric field lines represents the field's strength, which is stronger closer to the charge and weaker as the distance increases. Correct option is (1)

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true but Reason is false.
Assertion is false but Reason is true.

question: A small block of mass $5 g$ and charge $5 \mu C$ is placed on insulated, frictionless, inclined plane of angle $60^{\circ}$. An electric field is applied parallel to the inclined plane. If the block remains at rest, then the magnitude of electric  field is (take, $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$)
explanation:
The given situation is shown in the figure below. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952827/preptracker/questions/q_111866_explanation_1764952826568.png Since, block remains at rest on inclined plane, then $q E=m g \sin 60^{\circ}$
$\Rightarrow E=\dfrac{m g \sin 60^{\circ}}{q}$
Here,$q=5 \mu C=5 \times 10^{-6} C, m=5 g=5 \times 10^{-3} {~kg}$
and $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$
$\therefore E=\dfrac{5 \times 10^{-3} \times 10}{5 \times 10^{-6}} \times \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}=\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \times 10^{4} {~N} / {C}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Motion of charged particle/body in electrostatic field

question: A particle of mass $m$ and charge $q$ travelling with a velocity $v$ along the $X$ - axis enters a uniform electric field $E$ directed along the $Y$ - axis. What will be the trajectory of the particle?
explanation:
The given situation is shown below https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957637/preptracker/questions/q_111865_explanation_1764957636854.png Acceleration of the charge particle in electric field direction is
$a_{y}=\dfrac{F}{m}=\dfrac{q E}{m}$
Time taken by the charged particle to cross the field, $t=x / v$
Along the field $u_{y}=0, a_{y}=\dfrac{q E}{m}, s_{y}=y, t \dfrac{x}{v}$
From $s_{y}=u_{y} t+\dfrac{1}{2} a_{y} t^{2}$
$y=u_{y} t+\dfrac{1}{2} a_{y} t^{2}$
$=0 \times t+\dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{q E}{m}\right)\left(\dfrac{x}{v}\right)^{2}$
$\Rightarrow y=\dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{q E x^{2}}{m v^{2}}$
This equation is in the form of $y=k x^{2}$, which represents the equation of parabola.
subject: Physics
subtopic: Motion of charged particle/body in electrostatic field

question: A simple pendulum consists of a small sphere of mass $m$ suspended by a thread of length $l$. The sphere carries a positive charge $q$. The pendulum is allowed to do small oscillations in uniform electric field $E$ with direction vertically upwards. The time period of oscillation is
explanation:
The given situation is shown below. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952814/preptracker/questions/q_111862_explanation_1764952814404.png Effective gravitational acceleration $g_{\text {eff }}=\left(g-\dfrac{q E}{m}\right)$
$\therefore \quad T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g_{\text {eff }}}}=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{g-\dfrac{q E}{m}}}$

question: A clock dial has point charges $-q,-2 q,-3 q \ldots -12 q$ at the positions of the corresponding numbers on the dial respectively. The time at which the hour's hand points the direction of   the net electric field at the centre of the dial is (Assume clock hands do not influence the net electric field)
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952810/preptracker/questions/q_111861_explanation_1764952809905.png Electric field is given by
$E=k q / r^{2} \Rightarrow E \propto q$ Let's take a pair of charges $( - 9q$ and $ - 3q)$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952811/preptracker/questions/q_111861_explanation_1764952810710.png The value of $E$ due to $-9 q>$ that of $-3 q$ and net magnitude $=-6 q \propto E_{0}$ Same goes for all pairs with magnitude of $( - 6\,q).$
The angle between each field,
$\theta=\dfrac{360^{\circ}}{12}=30^{\circ}$
From the property of vector addition, if two equal vector is separated by $120^{\circ}$, then resultant vector is same as initial vector.
For making $120^{\circ}$, take vector 1 to vector 5 Resultant of vector 1 and $5=E_{0}=$ Vector 3 direction. Resultant of vector 2 and $6=E_{0}=$
Vector 4 direction Now, vector (1 and 5) and (2 and 6) are eliminated
Now, vector $3=E_{0}+E_{0}=2 E_{0}$
Vector $4=E_{0}+E_{0}=2 E_{0}$
Vector 3 denotes 10 AM Vector 4 denotes 9 AM https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952811/preptracker/questions/q_111861_explanation_1764952811451.png So, the resultant vector of 9 AM and 10 AM is in midway direction.
$i.e.,$ $9: 30$.

subtopic: Electric field due to two/multiple charges

question: A large metal plate has a surface charge density of $8.85 \times 10^{-6} {C} / {m}^{2}$.   An electron having initial kinetic energy of $8 \times 10^{-17} J$ is moving towards the centre of the plate. If the electron stops just before reaching the plate,    then the initial distance between the electron and the plate is     [take$\left.\varepsilon_{0}=8.85 \times 10^{-12} C^{2} / N-m^{2}\right]$
explanation:
We know that, electric field due to metal plate is given by
$E=\sigma / \varepsilon_{0}$
$\therefore$ Force on electron in this electric field,
$F=-e E=-\dfrac{e \sigma}{\varepsilon_{0}}$
$\therefore$ Acceleration on electron, $a=\dfrac{F}{m}=-\dfrac{e \sigma}{m \varepsilon_{0}}$
Let $u$ be the initial velocity of electron, then
$v^{2}=u^{2}+2 a s$
but $v=0$
$\therefore \quad 0 = {u^2} + 2\left( {\frac{{ - e\sigma }}{{m{\varepsilon _0}}}} \right)s$ $ \Rightarrow s = \frac{{{u^2}{\varepsilon _0}m}}{{2e\sigma }}\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (1)$
but $K = \frac{1}{2}m{u^2} \Rightarrow {u^2} = \frac{{2K}}{m}\quad \quad \quad (2)$
From Eqs. (1) and (2), we get
$s=\dfrac{2 K \varepsilon_{0} m}{2 m e \sigma}=\dfrac{K \varepsilon_{0}}{e \sigma}$
$\Rightarrow \quad s=\dfrac{8 \times 10^{-17} \times 8.85 \times 10^{-12}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 8.85 \times 10^{-6}}$
$=5 \times 10^{-4} m=0.5 {~mm}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Motion of charged particle/body in electrostatic field

question: A particle of mass $m$ and charge $q$ is thrown perpendicular to an electric field of intensity $E$ with an initial velocity $v$. The particle moves a distance $x$ perpendicular to the field and a distance $y$ along the direction of the field. If $y=\alpha x^{2}$, then the value of $\alpha$ is given by
explanation:
$F=q E=m a \Rightarrow a=\dfrac{q E}{m}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957629/preptracker/questions/q_111851_explanation_1764957628839.png Using equation of motion $\left(s=u t+\dfrac{1}{2} a t^{2}\right)$ in the direction of electric field, we get
$y = {u_y}t + \frac{1}{2}{a_y}{t^2} = 0 + \frac{1}{2}\left( {\frac{{qE}}{m}} \right){t^2}$
$y = \frac{1}{2}\left( {\frac{{qE}}{m}} \right){t^2}{\rm{ }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Since, $x=u_{x} t$ along field direction$\Rightarrow x^{2}=u_{x}^{2} t^{2} \Rightarrow t^{2}=x^{2} / u_{x}^{2}=\dfrac{x^{2}}{v^{2}}$
Substituting in Eq. (1), we get
$y=\dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{q E}{m}\right)\left(\dfrac{x^{2}}{v^{2}}\right)$
$\therefore y=\left(\dfrac{q E}{2 m v^{2}}\right) x^{2}$
$\Rightarrow a=\dfrac{q E}{2 m v^{2}}$

So, correct option is (2).

question: A cube of side $L$ has point charges $+q$ located at its seven vertices and $-q$ at remaining one vertex. The electric field at its centre is found to be $|E|=\alpha\left(\dfrac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} L^{2}}\right)$. The magnitude of constant $\alpha$ is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957625/preptracker/questions/q_111845_explanation_1764957625433.png diagonal length of a cube is $\sqrt{3} L$.
$\therefore$ Centre to corner distance is equal to half the diagonal length in the cube
$\Rightarrow r=\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} L$
The Electric field due to diagonally opposite charges is opposite and equal in the magnitude.
Hence they cancel each other.
The electric fields due to diagonally opposite corner charges $-q \&+q$ are in the same direction.
$\therefore$ They are only added to give resultant
$\therefore E=E_{-q}+E_{+q}=\dfrac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \dfrac{q}{r^{2}}+\dfrac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \dfrac{q}{r^{2}}$
$\Rightarrow E=2\left(\dfrac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \dfrac{q}{r^{2}}\right)^{2}=2\left[\dfrac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \dfrac{q}{\left[\dfrac{\sqrt{3} L}{2}\right]^{2}}\right]$
$\Rightarrow E=\dfrac{8}{3}\left[\dfrac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \dfrac{q}{L^{2}}\right]$. Thus $\alpha=\dfrac{8}{3}$

question: A point charge of magnitude $50 \mu C$ is placed in the $X Y$-plane at a location with radius vector $r_{0}=(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}) {m}$. The electric field strength and its magnitude at a point with radius vector $r=(8 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}) m$ is $\left(\varepsilon_{0}=8.85 \times 10^{-12} C^{2} / N-m^{2}\right)$
explanation:
As $x = r - {r_0} = 8\hat i - 5\hat j - (2\hat i + 3\hat j) = 6\hat i - 8\hat j$
$|x|\,\, = \sqrt {{6^2} + {8^2}} \Rightarrow \,\,\,|x|\,\, = 10\,m$
$\therefore \quad $ Electric field,
$E = \frac{{Kq}}{{{x^2}}} = \frac{{9 \times {{10}^9} \times 50 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}}}{{10 \times 10}}$
$ = 4.5 \times {10^3}\,V/m = 4.5\,\,kV/m$

question: The figure shows an electron entering the space between the plates of a parallel plate capacitor with an initial velocity, ${u_x} = {10^6}\,m/s$ parallel to the plates. If the length of plates is $l = 10\,cm$ and the electric field in the region $E = 9.1\,N{\rm{ }}/{\rm{ }}C$, then the value of $v_{y}$ when the electron comes out of the plates is (Electronic mass $ = 9.1 \times {10^{ - 31}}\,kg$)
explanation:
Time inside the electric field,
$t = \frac{l}{{{v_x}}}\quad \left( {\because R = {u_x}T} \right)$
Acceleration of electron along $Y$-axis,
$a_{y}=\dfrac{e E}{m}$
Velocity $v_{y}=u_{y}+a_{y} t$,
$v_{y}=0+a_{y} t,\left(\because u_{y}=0\right)$
${v_y} = {a_y}t. = \frac{{1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 19}} \times 9.1 \times 10 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}}{{9.1 \times {{10}^{ - 31}} \times {{10}^6}}}\,m/s$
$ = 1.6 \times {10^5}\,m/s$

question: Two charges $q$ and $3 q$ are separated by a distance $'r'$ in air. At a distance $x$ from charge $q$, the resultant electric field is zero. The value of $x$ is,
explanation:
Let at point $C$, the electric field is zero.
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955538/preptracker/questions/q_32139_explanation_1764955538482.png
Thus, $\dfrac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} x^{2}}-\dfrac{3 q}{\left(4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}\right)(r-x)^{2}}=0$
$(r-x)^{2}=3 x^{2} ; r-x=\sqrt{3} x$
$\Rightarrow x=\dfrac{r}{\sqrt{3}+1}$

question: The vehicles carrying inflammable fluids usually have metallic chains touching the ground,
explanation:
To avoid the inflammable fluids from catching fire, metallic chains are suspended on the vehicles. Metallic chains are connected to the earth which causes earthing and prevents accidents due to sparking.
Sparking is caused by friction which induces positive charge and negative charge simultaneously on the surface of bodies in contact. Metallic chains provide a path to the charge induced on the surface of vehicle and transfer it to the earth via earthing.

to conduct excess charge due to air friction to ground and prevent sparking.

question: A ${\operatorname{rod} A B}$ of length ${L}$ and mass ${m}$ is uniformly charged with a charge ${Q}$, and it is suspended from end ${A}$ as shown in figure. The rod can freely rotate about ${A}$ in the plane of the figure. An electric field ${E}$ is suddenly switched on in the horizontal direction due to which the rod gets turned by a maximum angle of ${90^{\circ}}$. The magnitude of ${E}$ is equal to ${n M g / Q}$. Find the value of ${n}$.
explanation: After turning through $90^{\circ}$, the rod comes at rest momentarily. So there is no change in KE. Net work done by all the forces should be zero. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764949903/preptracker/questions/q_32021_explanation_1764949902293.png $Q E \dfrac{L}{2}-M g \dfrac{L}{2}=0 \quad \text { or } \quad E=\dfrac{M g}{Q}$

question: A non-conducting ring of mass ${m}$ and radius ${R}$ is charged as shown in figure and placed on a rough horizontal nonconducting plane. The charge per unit length on the charged quadrants of ring is ${\lambda}$. At time ${t=0}$, a uniform electric field ${\vec{E}=E_{0} \hat{i}}$ is switched on and the ring starts rolling without sliding.If ${E_{0}=15 {~N} / {C}, R=0.50 {~m}}$ and ${\lambda=2 {C} / {m}}$, then find the magnitude of friction force (in N ) acting on the ring when it starts rolling.
explanation:
We consider an angular element as shown in figure. Force on element is
$d F=\lambda(R \cdot d \theta) \cdot E_{0}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955511/preptracker/questions/q_31974_explanation_1764955511509.png Perpendicular distance between two equal and opposite force pairs of $d F$ will be
$r=2 R \sin \theta$
torque on ring is
$d \tau=d F . r=2 \lambda R^{2} E_{0} \sin \theta \cdot d \theta$
$\Rightarrow \tau=\int_{d}^{\pi / 2} d \tau=2 \lambda R^{2} E_{0}$
These pair of forces will not provide net force but due to rotation tendency force of friction on ring is $f$ in forward direction as shown. For pure rolling to take place, we use
$a = R\alpha $
$ \Rightarrow \frac{f}{m} = R\left[ {\frac{{\tau - fR}}{{m{R^2}}}} \right]$
$ \Rightarrow f = \frac{\tau }{R} - f$
$ \Rightarrow f = \frac{\tau }{{2R}} = \lambda R{E_0}$
$ = 2 \times 0.5 \times 15 = 15\,N$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Motion of charged particle/body in electrostatic field

question: A simple pendulum has a bob of mass ${m=40 {~g}}$ and a positive charge ${q=12 \mu {C}}$. It makes 20 oscillations in 45$s$ . A vertical upward electric field of magnitude ${E=2.5 \times 10^{4} {~N} / {C}}$ is switched on in space, find the time period of oscillation of the simple pendulum.
explanation:
Figure shows the simple pendulum and the forces acting on the its bob in presence of electric field. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764949896/preptracker/questions/q_31966_explanation_1764949895774.png Initially when no electric field is present, time period of pendulum is given as
$T = 2\,\pi \sqrt {\frac{L}{g}} = \frac{{45}}{{20}}\,\,\,s{\text{ }}\quad \quad (1)$
After switching on the electric field in the region effective gravity on bob will be given as$g_{\text {eff }}=g-\dfrac{q E}{m}$
Thus, using effective gravity as given above, the new time period of oscillation is given as
$T' = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{{ \cdot L}}{{\left( {g - \frac{{qE}}{m}} \right)}}} \quad \quad (2)$
Dividing Eqs. (1) and (2), we get
$\frac{T}{{T'}} = \sqrt {\frac{{g - \frac{{qE}}{m}}}{g}} = \sqrt {1 - \frac{{qE}}{{mg}}} $
$ = \sqrt {1 - \frac{{12 \times {{10}^{ - 6}} \times 2.5 \times {{10}^4}}}{{40 \times {{10}^{ - 3}} \times 10}}} = 0.5$
$ \Rightarrow T' = 2T = 2 \times \frac{{45}}{{20}} = 4.5\,\,s$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Motion of charged particle/body in electrostatic field

question: An insulating rod with linear charge density of $40\,\mu C/m$ and linear mass density of ${0.1 {~kg} / {m}}$ is released from rest in a uniform electric field ${E=100 {~V} / {m}}$ directed perpendicular to the rod. If rod is released and after travelling $2\,m$ down the field, speed of rod is formed to be $(v/10)\,m/s$, then value of ${v}$ is
explanation:
Taking ${V=0}$ at initial position or rod, then potential after '${d}$' distance along the field ${=V=-E d}$
Charge on ${\operatorname{rod}=\lambda L}$
Potential energy of rod - field system initially ${Q V_{\text {initial }}=0}$
Potential energy of rod-field system finally
$=-\lambda L E d \text { (using, } U=q V \text { ) }$
Now using energy conservation,
$({KE}+{PE})_{\text {initial }}=({KE}+{PE})_{\text {final }}$
We get, ${0=0=\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}-\lambda L E d}$
$ \Rightarrow \quad {v^2} = \frac{{2\lambda LEd}}{m} = \frac{{2\lambda LEd}}{{\mu L}} = \frac{{2\lambda Ed}}{\mu }$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} v = \sqrt {\frac{{2\lambda Ed}}{\mu }} $
$ = \sqrt {\left( {\frac{{2 \times 40 \times {{10}^{ - 6}} \times 100 \times 2}}{{0.1}}} \right)} $
$ = 0.400\,m/s = \frac{4}{{10}}\,m/s$
So, ${\quad v=4}$

question: A point charge $q$ produces an electric field of magnitude $2N{C^{ - 1}}$ at a point distance $0.25\,m$ from it. What is the value of charge?

question: A charged oil drop is suspended in uniform field of $3 \times {10^4}\,V/m$, so that it neither falls nor rises. The charge on the drop will be (take, the mass of the charge $ = 9.9 \times {10^{ - 15}}\,kg$ and $g = 10\,m/{s^2})$

$\Rightarrow \quad q=\dfrac{m g}{E}$

$ = 3.3 \times {10^{ - 18}}C$

question: A point charge ${q=36 \mu C}$ is located in the ${X-Y}$ plane at the point with position vector ${\vec{r}_{0}=2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+\hat{k}}$. What is the electric field vector (in ${{kV} / {m}}$ ) at the point with position vector ${\vec{r}=8 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}+\hat{k}}$

question: Electric field in a certain region is given by $\vec{E}=\left(\dfrac{A}{x^{2}} \hat{i}+\dfrac{B}{y^{3}} \hat{j}\right)$. The SI unit of $A$ and $B$ are
explanation:
Since, $\vec{E}=\dfrac{\vec{F}}{q},\left(\dfrac{N}{C}\right)$ units
Now $\left[\dfrac{A}{x^{2}}\right]=\left[\dfrac{B}{y^{3}}\right]=[E]$
Therefore, SI unit of $A$ is $N m^{2} C^{-1}$ and $S I$ unit of $B$ is $N{m^3}{C^{ - 1}}$

explanation:

${m_e} < {m_P}$

question: A toy car with charge $q$ moves on a frictionless horizontal plane surface under the influence of a uniform electric field ${\vec E}$ . Due to the force $q\vec E$, its velocity increases from 0 to 6 $m$/$s$ in one  second duration. At that instant the direction of  the field is reverced. The car continues to move for two more seconds under the influence of  this field. The average velocity and the average speed of the toy car between 0 to 3 seconds are  respectively
explanation:
$v = u + at$
$a = \frac{{v - u}}{t} = \frac{{6 - 0}}{1} = 6\,m/{s^2}$
${S_1} = ut + \frac{1}{2}a{t^2} = 0 + \frac{1}{2} \times 6{(1)^2} = 6 - 3 = 3\,m$
If direction of Electric field is reversed,displacement in next 1 $sec$
${S_2} = vt - \frac{1}{2}a{t^2} = 6 \times 1 - \frac{1}{2} \times 6{(1)^2} = 6 - 3 = 3\,m$
Total displacement $ = {S_1} - 2{S_2} = - 3m$
Average velocity $V = \frac{{\Delta S}}{{\Delta t}} = - 1\,m/s$
Total distance $ = {S_1} + 2{S_2} = 9m$
Average speed $ = 3m/s$

question: An electron intially at rest falls a distance of $1.5\,cm$ in a uniform electric field of magnitude $2 \times {10^4}N{C^{ - 1}}$. The time taken by the electron to  fall this distance is
explanation:
The acceleration of electronin is ${a_e} = \frac{{eE}}{{{m_e}}}$
The time required by the electron to fall through a distance $h$ is
${t_e} = \sqrt {\left( {\frac{{2h}}{{{a_e}}}} \right)} = \sqrt {\frac{{2h{m_e}}}{{eE}}} $
$ = {\left[ {\frac{{2 \times 1.5 \times {{10}^{ - 2}} \times 9.11 \times {{10}^{ - 31}}}}{{1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 19}} \times 2 \times {{10}^4}}}} \right]^{1/2}} = 2.9 \times {10^{ - 9}}s$

question:
In the uniform electric field of $E = 1 \times {10^4}N{C^{ - 1}}$, an electron is accelerated from rest. The velocity of the electron when it has travelled a distance of $2 \times {10^{ - 2}}m$ is nearly ...... $m{s^{ - 1}}$
($e$/$m$ of electron $ = 1.8 \times {10^{11}}CK{g^{ - 1}}$)
explanation:
Force on electron in uniform electric field is $F = eE$
The acceleration of the electron is
$a = \frac{F}{m} = \frac{{eE}}{m}$
Where $m$ is the mass of the electron.Starting from rest, the velocity of the electron when it has travelled distance $s$ is
$v = \sqrt {2as} = \sqrt {2\frac{{eE}}{m}s} $
Here,$\frac{e}{m} = 1.8 \times {10^{11}}\,C\,k{g^{ - 1}}$
$E = 1 \times {10^4}N{C^{ - 1}}\,{\rm{and}}\,s = 2 \times {10^2}m$
$\therefore \,\,v = \sqrt {2 \times 1.8 \times {{10}^{11}} \times 1 \times {{10}^4} \times 2 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}} $
$ = 8.5 \times {10^6}m{s^{ - 1}}$

question: An electron of mass $m$ and charge $q$ is  accelerated from rest in a uniform electric field  of strength $E$. The velocity acquired by it as it  travels a distance $l$ is
explanation:
The magnitude of force on a charge $q$ by an electric field is given by
$F = qE\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
From Newton’s second law, we have
$F = ma\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
From eq.(1) and (2), we get
$a = \frac{{qE}}{m}$
Since, the electron starts accelerating from rest, its initial velocity is zero.
Using equation of motion,
${v^2} - {u^2} = 2al$
$ \Rightarrow {v^2} - 0 = 2\left( {\frac{{qE}}{m}} \right)l$
$ \Rightarrow v = \sqrt {\frac{{2Eql}}{m}} = {\left[ {\frac{{2Eql}}{m}} \right]^{1/2}}$

question: The tracks of three charged particles in a  uniform electrostatic field is shown in the figure.  Which particle has the highest charge to mass  ratio?
explanation:
Particles $A$ and $B$ have negative charges because they are being deflected towards the positive plate of the electrostatic field. Particle $C$ has positive charge because it is being deflected towards the negative plate.
Deflection of charged particle in time $t$ in $y$- direction
$h = 0 \times t + \frac{1}{2}a{t^2} = \frac{1}{2}\frac{{qE}}{m}{t^2}$
$i.e.,\,h = \propto q{\rm{/}}m$
As the particle $C$ suffers maximum deflection in $y$-direction, so it has highest charge to mass $q/m$ ratio.
subject: Physics
subtopic: Motion of charged particle/body in electrostatic field

question: A simple pendulum has a length $l$ and the mass of the bob is $m$. The bob is given a charge $q$ coulomb. The pendulum is suspended between the vertical plates of a charged parallel plate capacitor. If $E$ is the electric field strength between the plates, the time period of the pendulum is given by
explanation:
Time period of a simple pendulum,
$T = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{l}{g}} $
Acceleration due to electric field, $a\,\, = \frac{{qE}}{m}$ is acting horizontally.
Acceleration due to gravity is acting vertically. Therefore effective acceleration,
$g' = \sqrt {{g^2} + {a^2}} = \sqrt {{g^2} + {{\left( {\frac{{qE}}{m}} \right)}^2}} $
$\therefore \,\,\,\,\,T = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{l}{{\sqrt {{g^2} + {{\left( {\frac{{qE}}{m}} \right)}^2}} }}} $
subject: Physics
subtopic: Motion of charged particle/body in electrostatic field

question: Under the action of a given coulombic force the acceleration of an electron is $2.5 \times {10^{22}}m{s^{ - 2}}$.Then the magnitude of the acceleration of a proton under the action of same force is nearly
explanation:
The acceleration of the electron due to given coulombic force $F$ is
${a_e} = \frac{F}{{{m_e}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
where ${m_e}$ is the mass of the electron.
The acceleration of the proton due to some force $F$ is
${a_p} = \frac{F}{{{m_p}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
where ${m_p}$ is the mass of the proton.
Divide (2) by (1), we get
$\frac{{a{}_p}}{{{a_e}}} = \frac{{{m_e}}}{{{m_p}}}$
$\therefore {a_p} = \frac{{{a_e}{m_e}}}{{{m_p}}} = \frac{{\left( {2.5 \times {{10}^{22}}m{s^{ - 2}}} \right)\left( {9.1 \times {{10}^{ - 31}}kg} \right)}}{{\left( {1.67 \times {{10}^{ - 27}}kg} \right)}}$
$ = 13.6 \times {10^{18}}m{s^{ - 2}} \approx 1.4 \times {10^{19}}m{s^{ - 2}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Motion of charged particle/body in electrostatic field

question: Two point charges of $1\,\mu C\,{\rm{and}}\, - 1\,\mu C$ are separated by a distance of $100\,\mathop A\limits^ \circ  $. A point $P$ is at a distance of $10\,cm$ from the midpoint and on the perpendicular bisector of the line joining the two charges. The electric field at $P$ will be
explanation:
The point lies on equatorial line of a short dipole.
$\therefore E = \frac{{ql}}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}{r^3}}} = \frac{{9 \times {{10}^9} \times {{10}^{ - 6}} \times {{10}^{ - 8}}}}{{{{\left( {{{10}^{ - 1}}} \right)}^3}}}$
$ = 9 \times {10^{ - 2}}N{C^{ - 1}}$

question: Two equal and opposite charges of masses ${m_{1\,}}\,{\rm{and}}\,{m_2}$ are accelerated in an uniform electric field through the same distance. What is the ratio of their accelerations if their ratio of masses is $\frac{{{m_1}}}{{{m_2}}} = 0.5?$
explanation:
Magnitude of force is same for both.
$\therefore \,{m_1}{a_1} = {m_2}{a_2}$
$\frac{{{a_1}}}{{{a_2}}} = \frac{{{m_2}}}{{{m_1}}} = \frac{1}{{0.5}} = 2$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Motion of charged particle/body in electrostatic field

question: A 2 gram object located in a region of uniform electric field  $\overrightarrow E  = (300\,N{C^{ - 1}})\hat i$ carries a charge $Q$. The object released from rest at $x = 0$, has a kinetic energy of $0.12\,J$ at $x = 0.5\,m$. Then $Q$ is
explanation:
Using work energy theorem and equating the work done by the electric field and change in kinetic energy,
$QE({x_2} - {x_1}) = ({K_2} - {K_1}) \Rightarrow Q = \frac{{({K_2} - {K_1})}}{{E({x_2} - {x_1})}}$
Hence
$Q = \frac{{\left( {0.12 - 0} \right)}}{{300 \times \left( {0.5 - 0} \right)}} = 8 \times {10^{ - 4}}C = 800\mu C$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Motion of charged particle/body in electrostatic field

question: A particle of mass  $m$ and charge $ - q$ enters the region between the two charged plates initially moving along $x$-axis with speed  ${v_x}$ as shown in figure. The length of plate is $L$ and a uniform electric field $E$ is maintained between the plates.The vertical deflection of the particle at the far edge of the plate is
explanation:
Here, in the vertical direction,initial velocity, $v = 0$
acceleration,$a = \frac{F}{m} = \frac{{qE}}{m}$ $(1)\,\,\,\,\,\left( {\because F = qE} \right)$
Time taken to cross the field,
$t = \frac{{dis\tan ce}}{{velocity}} = \frac{L}{{{v_x}}}$
( $\therefore $ velocity along the horizontal direction is constant)
Now,$s = vt + \frac{1}{2}a{t^2}$
$\therefore $ deflection,$y = 0 + \frac{1}{2}\left( {\frac{{qE}}{m}} \right){\left( {\frac{L}{{{v_x}}}} \right)^2}$
[using (1) and (2)]
$\therefore y = \frac{{qE{L^2}}}{{2mv_x^2}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Motion of charged particle/body in electrostatic field

question: An electron of mass $m$, charge $e$ falls through a distance $h$ meter in a uniform electric field $E$. Then time of fall
explanation:
Acceleration of the electron $a = \frac{{eE}}{m}$
Time taken to all through height $h$,
$t = \sqrt {\frac{{2h}}{a}} = \sqrt {\frac{{2mh}}{{eE}}} $

question: The electric field lines on the left have twice the separation on those on the right as shown in figure. If the magnitude of the field at $A$ is $40\,V\,{m^{ - 1}}$, what is the force on 20 $mC$ charge kept at $B$ ?
explanation:
Given ${E_A} = 40\,V{m^{ - 1}}$
$\,{\text{and}}\,q = 20\,mC = 20 \times {10^{ - 6}}C$
Force on 20 $mC$ charge at $A$,
${F_A} = 20 \times {10^{ - 6}} \times 40 = 800 \times {10^{ - 6}}$
$ = 8 \times {10^{ - 4}}N$
Electric field $ \propto $ Number of field lines per unit area.
$\frac{{{E_A}}}{{{E_{\bf{B}}}}} = \frac{2}{1} \Rightarrow {E_B} = \frac{{{E_A}}}{2}$
Force on 20 $mC$ charge at $B$,
${F_B} = q{E_B} = \frac{{q{E_A}}}{2} = \frac{1}{2} \times 8 \times {10^{ - 4}}$
$ = 4 \times {10^{ - 4}}N$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Electric Field Lines

question: Four point charges $ + q, + q, - q\,{\rm{and}}\, - q$ are placed on the corners of a square of side length ‘$a$’ as shown in the figure. The magnitude of the  electric field at a point which is at a distance $x( >  > a)$ from the centre along a line perpendicular  to the plane of the square and passing through  the centre is,
explanation:
The given configuration of charges is combination of two dipoles and the point where the electric field required is on their equatorial line
$E = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{p}{{{x^3}}}{\rm{(For}}\,{\rm{each}}\,{\rm{dipole)}}$
As the dipoles are perpendicular to each other
${E_{Net}} = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{{\sqrt 2 p}}{{{x^3}}}(p = \sqrt 2 aq)$
$ = \sqrt 2 \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _o}}}\frac{{q \times \sqrt 2 a}}{{{x^3}}} = \frac{{qa}}{{2\pi {\varepsilon _o}{x^3}}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Electric field due to two/multiple charges

question: There is a uniform electric field of strength ${10^3}V/m$ along $y$-axis. A body of mass $1g$ and ${10^{ - 6}}C$ charge  is projected into the field from origin along the positive $x$-axis with a velocity $10\,m/s$. Its speed in $m/s$ after 10 $s$ is (Neglect gravitation)
explanation:
Body moves along the parabolic path. For motion along $y$ - axis: By using $v = u + at$
$ \Rightarrow {v_y} = 0 + \frac{{QE}}{m}.\,\,\,t = \frac{{{{10}^{ - 6}} \times {{10}^3}}}{{{{10}^{ - 3}}}} \times 10 = 10m/\sec $
For motion along $x$ - axis. Its horizontal velocity remains the same i.e.after 10 sec, horizontal velocity of body ${v_x} = 10\,m/\sec $
Velocity after 10 sec
$v = \sqrt {v_x^2 + v_y^2} = 10\sqrt 2 \,{\rm{m}}/\sec $

question: Four point charges are placed at the corners of a square with diagonal 2$a$ as shown. What is the total electric field at the center of the square?
explanation:
At center electric field due to both 4$q$ will be canceled. Now net field at center is
$E = {E_1} - {E_2}$
$ = \frac{{k2q}}{{{{(a)}^2}}} - \frac{{kq}}{{{{(a)}^2}}} = \frac{{kq}}{{{{(a)}^2}}}$
This is ${45^0}$ below negative $x$-axis

options.text: $kq/{a^2}$ at an angle ${45^0}$ above the $ + x$-axis
$kq/{a^2}$ at an angle ${45^0}$ below the $ - x$-axis
$4kq/{a^2}$  at an angle ${45^0}$ above the $ - x$-axis

question: Two charges each of magnitude $Q$ are fixed at  each of two opposite corners of a square. Two  other charges each of magnitude $q$ are placed  at each of the other two corners. If the resultant  electric force on $Q$ is zero, then $Q$ and $q$ are  related as
explanation:
The charges are placed as shown in the figure.
The net force on Q is zero
${F_1} = \left( {{F_2}\cos 45^\circ + {F_3}\cos 45^\circ } \right)$
$\frac{{{Q^2}}}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}{{\left( {\sqrt 2 l} \right)}^2}}} = 2\left[ {\frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}\frac{{Qq}}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}{l^2}}}} \right]$
$\left| Q \right| = 2\sqrt 2 q \Rightarrow Q = - 2\sqrt 2 q$

question: A point charge of $100\,\mu C$ is placed at $3\widehat i + 4\widehat j\,m$. Find the electric field intensity due to this charge at a point located at $9\widehat i + 12\widehat j\,m$.
explanation:
$E = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{q}{{{{\left| r \right|}^2}}}$
$\overrightarrow r = (9 - 3)\widehat i + (12 - 4)\widehat j = 6\widehat i + 8\widehat j$
$ \Rightarrow r = \sqrt {{6^2} + {8^2}} = 10m$
$E = \frac{{9 \times {{10}^9} \times 100 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}}}{{{{10}^2}}} = 9000\,V{m^{ - 1}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Electric field due to a point charge

question: An oil drop, carrying six electronic charges and having a mass of $1.6 \times {10^{ - 12}}g$ , falls with some terminal velocity in a medium. What magnitude of vertical electric field is required to make the drop move upward with the same speed as it was formerly moving downward with? Ignore buoyancy.
explanation:
Viscous force $f = 6\pi nrv$ acts on the oil drop
Case (i) $f = mg$ (1)
Case (ii) $6eE = f + mg$ (2)
From the above two equations
$E = \frac{{mg}}{{3e}} = \frac{{1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 15}} \times 10}}{{3 \times 1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 19}}}} = 3.3 \times {10^4}N{C^{ - 1}}$
subject: Physics

The field at a distance $x$ from the centre is
$E = \frac{q}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _o}{x^2}}} = \frac{6}{{{x^2}}}(SI\,units)$

question: In the electric field shown in figure, the electric  lines in the left have twice the separation as that  between those on right. If the magnitude of the  field at point $A$ is $40\,N{C^{ - 1}}$ then the force  experienced by a proton placed at point $B$ is
explanation:
As the field lines do not cross each other the electric flux at $A$ and $B$ are same.
$\left( {{E_A}} \right)A = \left( {{E_B}} \right)2A$
${E_A} = 2{E_B} \Rightarrow {E_B} = 20N{C^{ - 1}}$
${F_B} = q{E_B} = 20 \times 1.6 \times {10^{ - 19}}N$
$ = 3.2 \times {10^{ - 18}}N$

question: A block of mass $m$ and charge $q$ is placed on a smooth horizontal table which terminates in a vertical wall at a distance $d$ from the block. A horizontal electric field $E$ towards right is switched on. Assume elastic collision, find time period of resulting oscillatory motion.
explanation:

$a = \frac{{qE}}{m}\,and\,\,d = \frac{1}{2}a{t^2}$ or $t = \sqrt {\frac{{2d}}{a}} = \sqrt {\frac{{2dm}}{{qE}}} $
Time period
$T = 2t = 2\sqrt {\frac{{2dm}}{{qE}}} = \sqrt {\frac{{8dm}}{{qE}}} $

question: Two infinitely long parallel wires having linear charge densities ${\lambda _1}\,{\rm{and}}\,{\lambda _2}$ respectively are placed at a distance of $R$ metres. The force per unit length on either wire will be $\left( {k = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}} \right)$
explanation:
The force on $l$ length of the wire 2 is
${F_2} = Q{E_1} = ({\lambda _2}{\rm{l}})\frac{{2k{\lambda _1}}}{R} \Rightarrow \frac{{{F_2}}}{{\rm{l}}} = \frac{{2k{\lambda _1}{\lambda _2}\,}}{R}$

question: A positive point charge $50\,\mu C$ is located in the plane $XY$ at a point with radius vector $\overrightarrow {{r_0}}  = 2\widehat i + 3\widehat j$ . The electric field vector at $\overrightarrow E $ a  point with radius vector $\overrightarrow r  = 8\widehat i - 5\widehat j$ ,  where ${r_0}$ and $r$ are expressed in meter, is
explanation:
${\rm{As}}\,\,\,\overrightarrow E = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{q}{{{{\left| {\vec r - \overrightarrow {{r_0}} } \right|}^3}}}(\overrightarrow r - \overrightarrow {{r_0}} )$
$\overrightarrow r - \overrightarrow {{r_0}} = (8\widehat i - 5\widehat j) - (2\widehat i + 3\widehat j)$
$ = (6\widehat i - 8\widehat j)$
$\left| {\overrightarrow r - \overrightarrow {{r_0}} } \right| = \sqrt {{6^2} + {8^2}} = 10\,m$
$\overrightarrow E = 9 \times {10^9} \times \frac{{50 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}}}{{{{(10)}^3}}}(6\widehat i - 8\widehat j)$
$\overrightarrow E = (2.7\widehat i - 3.6\widehat j)kN{C^{ - 1}}$

question: An $\alpha $ particle of mass $6.4 \times {10^{ - 27}}kg$ and charge $3.2 \times {10^{ - 19}}C$ is situated in a uniform electric  field of $1.6 \times {10^5}V{m^{ - 1}}$. The velocity of the   particle at the end of $2 \times {10^{ - 2}}m$ path when it  starts from rest is
explanation:
Here, mass of a particle ${m_a} = 6.4 \times {10^{ - 27}}kg$
Charge of a particle ${q_a} = 3.2 \times {10^{ - 19}}C$
Electric field $E = 1.6 \times {10^5}V{m^{ - 1}}$
Force on a particle in a uniform electric field $E$:
$F = {q_a}\,E = 3.2 \times {10^{ - 19}} \times 1.6 \times {10^5}N$
$ = 3.2 \times 1.6 \times {10^{ - 14}}N$
Acceleration of the a particle is
$a = \frac{F}{{{m_\alpha }}} = \frac{{3.2 \times 1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 14}}}}{{6.4 \times {{10}^{ - 27}}}}m{s^{ - 2}}$
Given :$u = 0$
${v^2} = 2as\frac{{2 \times 3.2 \times 1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 14}} \times 2 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}}{{6.4 \times {{10}^{ - 27}}}}$
(As $s = 2 \times {10^{ - 2}}m$)
$ = 3.2 \times {10^{11}} = 32 \times {10^{10}}{m^2}{s^2}$
${\rm{or}}\,v = 4\sqrt 2 \times {10^5}m{s^{ - 1}}$
Alternate solution :
According to work energy theorem,
$\frac{1}{2}{m_\alpha }{v^2} - \frac{1}{2}{m_\alpha }{(0)^2} = {q_\alpha }ES$
or $\frac{1}{2}{m_\alpha }{v^2} = {q_\alpha }ES$ or $v = \sqrt {\frac{{2{q_\alpha }ES}}{{{m_\alpha }}}} $
$v = \frac{{2 \times 3.2 \times {{10}^{ - 19}} \times 1.6 \times {{10}^5} \times 2 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}}{{6.4 \times {{10}^{ - 27}}}}$
$v = \sqrt {3.2 \times {{10}^{11}}} = \sqrt {32 \times {{10}^{10}}} $
$ = 4\sqrt 2 \times {10^5}\,m/s$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Motion of charged particle/body in electrostatic field

question:
An electron is moving with the speed $5 \times {10^6}\,m{\rm{ }}{s^{ - 1}}$ is shooted parallel to the electric field of intensity $1 \times {10^3}\,N\,{C^{ - 1}}$. Field is responsible for the retardation of motion of electron. Now evalute the distance travelled by the electron before coming to rest
$\left( {{m_e} = 9.1 \times {{10}^{ - 31}}kg;{\rm{ }}e = 1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 19}}\,C} \right)$
explanation:
The distance travelled by the electron before coming to rest is
$d = \frac{{{u^2}}}{{2a}}$
$u = 5 \times {10^6}m/s$
$a = \frac{{Eq}}{m} = \frac{{{{10}^3} \times 1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 19}}}}{{9.1 \times {{10}^{ - 31}}}}$
$ \Rightarrow d = 7.1cm$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Motion of charged particle/body in electrostatic field

question: An electron initially at rest falls a distance of   1.5 $cm$ in a uniform electric field of  magnitude $2 \times {10^4}N\,{C^{ - 1}}$. The time taken by the  electron to fall this distance is
explanation:
In figure the field is upward. So the negatively charged electron experiences a downward force.
$\therefore $ The acceleration of electron is ${a_e} = \frac{{eE}}{m}$ (1)
The time required by the electron to fall through a distance $h$ is
${t_e} = \sqrt {\left( {\frac{{2h}}{{{a_e}}}} \right)} = \sqrt {\frac{{2h{m_e}}}{{eE}}} $ (using (1))
$ = {\left[ {\frac{{2 \times 1.5 \times {{10}^{ - 2}} \times 9.11 \times {{10}^{ - 31}}}}{{1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 19}} \times 2 \times {{10}^4}}}} \right]^{1/2}} = 2.9 \times {10^{ - 9}}s$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Motion of charged particle/body in electrostatic field

question: A pendulum bob of mass $m$ carrying a charge $q$ is at rest with its string making an angle $\theta $ with the vertical in a uniform horizontal  electric field $E$. The tension in the string is :
explanation:
For the equilibrium of the bob,
$T\,\sin \theta = qE\,{\rm{and}}\,T\,\cos \theta = mg$
$T = \frac{{mg}}{{\cos \theta }}\,{\rm{and}}\,T = \frac{{qE}}{{\sin \theta }}$

question: In the following four situations charged particles are at equal distance from the origin. Arrange them the magnitude of the net electric field at origin greatest first
explanation:
If electric field due to charge $\left| q \right|$ at origin is $E$ then electric field due to charges $\left| {2q} \right|,\left| {3q} \right|,\left| {4q} \right|{\rm{and}}\left| {5q} \right|$ are respectively $2E,3E,4E\,{\rm{and}}\,5E$
${E_{\left( i \right)}} = \sqrt {{{\left( {5E} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {5E} \right)}^2}} = 5\sqrt 2 E\,,$
${E_{\left( {ii} \right)}} = \sqrt {{{\left( {3E} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {3E} \right)}^2}} = 3\sqrt 2 E\,$,
${E_{\left( {iii} \right)}} = 4E + 2E = 6E$ and
${E_{\left( {iv} \right)}} = 3E + E = 4E$
$ \Rightarrow {E_{\left( i \right)}} > {E_{\left( {iii} \right)}} > {E_{\left( {ii} \right)}} > {E_{\left( {iv} \right)}}$
subject: Physics

question: Two charges $Q$ and $ - 3Q$ are placed at some distance apart. If the electric field at the location of $Q$ is $E$ then at the locality of $ - 3Q$, it  is
explanation:
The field produced by charge $ - 3Q\,{\rm{at}}\,Q$ is $E$
$\therefore E = \frac{{3Q}}{{{x^2}}}$
Now field at location of $ - 3Q$ due to charge $Q$ will be $\therefore E' = \frac{Q}{{{x^2}}} = \frac{E}{3}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Electric field due to two/multiple charges

question: The magnitude of point charge due to which the electric field $30\,cm$ away has the magnitude $2\,N/C$ will be
explanation:
The magnitude of electric field due to a point  charge q at a distance r from the charge is
$E = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{q}{{{r^2}}}$
Here $E = 2\,N{C^{ - 1}},r = 30\,cm = 0.3\,m,$
$\frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}} = 9 \times {10^9}N{m^2}{C^{ - 2}},q = ?$
$\therefore 2 = \frac{{(9 \times {{10}^9})q}}{{{{(0.3{\mkern 1mu} )}^2}}}$
$ \Rightarrow {\mkern 1mu} q = \frac{{(2)(0.09)}}{{(9 \times {{10}^9})}}$
$ \Rightarrow {\mkern 1mu} q = \frac{{2 \times 9}}{{9 \times {{10}^9} \times 100}} = 2 \times {10^{ - 11}}C$
Thus, the magnitude of point charge will be
$2 \times {10^{ - 11}}C$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Electric field due to a point charge

question: Two particles $A$ and $B$ having equal charges are placed at a distance $d$ apart. A third charged particle placed on the perpendicular bisect or of $AB$ at distance $x$. The third particle experiences maximum force when
explanation:
Let $q$ is the charge of both charges
The resultant force on the charge ${q_0}$ is
${F_y} = 2F\cos \theta $
$ = \frac{{2q{q_0}}}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}\left( {{x^2} + \frac{{{d^2}}}{4}} \right)}}\left( {\frac{x}{{\sqrt {{x^2} + \frac{{{d^2}}}{4}} }}} \right)$
$ = \frac{{2q{q_0}}}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{x}{{{{\left( {{x^2} + \frac{{{d^2}}}{4}} \right)}^{3/2}}}}$
When ${F_y}$ is maximum
$\frac{{d{F_y}}}{{dx}} = 0$
$\frac{{d{F_y}}}{{dx}} = \frac{{2q{q_0}}}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\left[ {\frac{{{{\left( {{x^2} + \frac{{{d^2}}}{4}} \right)}^{3/2}}\frac{{d(x)}}{{dx}} - x\frac{d}{{dx}}{{\left( {{x^2} + \frac{{{d^2}}}{4}} \right)}^{3/2}}}}{{{{\left( {{x^2} + \frac{{{d^2}}}{4}} \right)}^3}}}} \right]$
As $\frac{{d{F_y}}}{{dx}} = 0$
${\left( {{x^2} + \frac{{{d^2}}}{4}} \right)^{3/2}} - x\left( {\frac{3}{2}} \right){\left( {{x^2} + \frac{{{d^2}}}{4}} \right)^{\frac{1}{2}}}(2x) = 0$
$ \Rightarrow x = \frac{d}{{2\sqrt 2 }}$

question: Two charges $ + 6\mu C$ and $ + 15\mu C$ are placed  along the $x$-axis at $x = 0$ and $x = 2m$ respectively.  A negative charge is placed between them such that the resultant force on it is zero.The negative charge is placed at
explanation:

$\therefore \frac{{\left( q \right) \times 6 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}}}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0} \times {x^2}}} = \frac{{\left( q \right) \times 15 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}}}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0} \times \left( {2 - {x^2}} \right)}}$
$\frac{6}{{{x^2}}} = \frac{{15}}{{{{(2 - x)}^2}}} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{x} = \pm \frac{{\sqrt 5 }}{{\sqrt 2 (2 - x)}}$
On solving, we get $x = 0.775m$

question: Match the terms related to electric field lines representation in Column I with their corresponding meanings in Column II and choose the correct options from the codes given below.
explanation:
A - Q : The electric field $(E)$ due to a point charge is represented pictorially with vectors whose lengths are proportional to the strength of the field at each point, decreasing inversely with the square of the distance from the charge.
B - R: The density of the electric field lines represents the magnitude of the electric field; where the field is strong, the lines are closer, and where it is weak, the lines are further apart.
$\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{S}$ : The direction of the electric field lines from a positive charge is radially outward, as indicated by the arrows pointing away from the charge in the illustrative figure.
D - P: The number of electric field lines that can be drawn in any region is theoretically infinite; what matters is the relative density of these lines in different regions. Correct option is (4)

question: The electric field lines on the left have twice the separation on those on the right as shown in figure. If the magnitude of the field at $A$ is $40\,V\,{m^{ - 1}}$, what is the force on 20 $mC$ charge kept at $B$ ?
explanation:
Given ${E_A} = 40\,V{m^{ - 1}}$
$\,{\text{and}}\,q = 20\,mC = 20 \times {10^{ - 6}}C$
Force on 20 $mC$ charge at $A$,
${F_A} = 20 \times {10^{ - 6}} \times 40 = 800 \times {10^{ - 6}}$
$ = 8 \times {10^{ - 4}}N$
Electric field $ \propto $ Number of field lines per unit area.
$\frac{{{E_A}}}{{{E_{\bf{B}}}}} = \frac{2}{1} \Rightarrow {E_B} = \frac{{{E_A}}}{2}$
Force on 20 $mC$ charge at $B$,
${F_B} = q{E_B} = \frac{{q{E_A}}}{2} = \frac{1}{2} \times 8 \times {10^{ - 4}}$
$ = 4 \times {10^{ - 4}}N$

question: The electric field in a region of space is given by, $\vec{E}=E_{0} \hat{i}+2 E_{0} \hat{j}$ where $E_{0}=100 N / C$. The flux of the field through a circular surface of radius $0.02\,m$ parallel to the $Y-Z$ plane is nearly:
explanation:
$\vec{E}=E_{0} \hat{i}+2 E_{0} \hat{j}$
Given, ${E_0} = 100\,N{\rm{/}}C$
$\Rightarrow \vec{E}=100 \hat{i}+200 \hat{j}$
Radius of circular surface $ = 0.02\,m$
${\rm{Area }} = \pi {r^2} = \frac{{22}}{7} \times 0.02 \times 0.02$
$ = 1.25 \times {10^{ - 3}}\hat i{m^2}$
[Loop is parallel to $Y$ - $Z$ plane]
Now, flux $(\phi)=E A \cos \theta$
But $\phi=0^{\circ}$
$\phi = (100\hat i + 200\hat j)\left( {1.25 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}\hat i} \right)$
$\phi = 125 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,N{m^2}{\rm{/}}C = 0.125\,N{m^2}{\rm{/}}C$

question: Match the physical systems and laws in Column I with their respective properties or outcomes in Column II and choose the correct options from the codes given below.
explanation:
$\mathrm{A}-\mathrm{Q}$ : Inside a charged conductor, the electric field is zero.
B - R: Free charge carriers in a conductor will experience force and drift when there is an electric field; this is prevented in a static situation by the charges distributing themselves so that the field inside is zero .
$\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{S}$ : According to Gauss's law, through the closed surface, electric flux is $\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon}$ times total charge enclosed by it.
D - P: The electrostatic field at the surface of a conductor must be normal to prevent any tangential movement of charges on the surface. Correct option is (1)

question: Match the concepts related to Gauss's Law in Column I with their corresponding descriptions in Column II and choose the correct options from the codes given below.
explanation:
A - R: Gauss' law states that the electric flux through a closed surface $S$ is equal to the total charge enclosed by the surface divided by $\varepsilon_{0}$, which is represented by the equation $\phi=\dfrac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}$.
$\mathrm{B}-\mathrm{Q}$ : The direction of the electric field at the left face of a cube where the field only has an $x$ component is axially symmetric along the direction from the charge towards the point considered, as the normal to a sphere at every point is along the radius vector from the center to the area element .
C - S: The Gaussian surface is the surface chosen for the application of Gauss's law. It is important that the Gaussian surface does not pass through any discrete charge due to undefined electric fields at the location of charges .
D - P: The total electric flux through a sphere is zero if no charge is enclosed by the surface. This is a direct consequence of Gauss's law, which relates to the symmetry of the electric field around a point charge and the uniformity of the flux through a closed surface. Correct option is (3)

options.text: A - S,       	B - Q , 	C - R, 	D - P
A - R, 	B - S, 	C - P, 	D - Q
A - R, 	B - Q ,	C - S,	D - P
A - P,  	B - R,	C - Q , 	D - S

question: Match the terms related to electric flux in Column I with their appropriate descriptions in Column II and choose the correct options from the codes given below.
explanation:
A - Q : Electric flux is defined analogously to the flow of a liquid through a surface, where the flow rate is given by the product of the velocity of the liquid and the area it passes through, adjusted for the angle between the flow direction and the normal to the surface .
B -R: The number of electric field lines crossing a unit area placed normal to the field at a point is a measure of the strength of the electric field at that point, analogous to the concentration of liquid flow lines indicating the flow rate in fluid dynamics.
$\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{S}$ : The angle between the electric field $(E)$ and the area element $(dS)$ affects the electric flux, with the flux being proportional to the cosine of the angle between them, which determines the effective area through which the field lines pass.
D - P: Gauss's law relates flux through a closed surface to enclosed charge and flux is given by $\phi=\dfrac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}$. Correct option is (4)

question: $6 \mu C$ charge is placed at the centre of a cube. What will be the electric flux at each face of the cube ?  $\left[ {{\text{Take}}\,\frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}} = 9 \times {{10}^9}N - {m^2}{C^{ - 2}}} \right]$
explanation:
According to Gauss's law, total electric flux passing through cube, $\phi=q / \varepsilon_{0}$
Since there are 6 faces in cube Hence electric flux passing through each face
$\phi_{1}=\dfrac{\phi}{6}=\dfrac{q}{6 \varepsilon_{0}} \Rightarrow \phi_{1}=\dfrac{q \times 4 \pi}{6 \varepsilon_{0} \times 4 \pi}$
$\Rightarrow \phi_{1}=\dfrac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \times \dfrac{4 \pi \varepsilon}{6}=\dfrac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \times \dfrac{2 \pi q}{3}$
$=\dfrac{9 \times 10^{9} \times 2 \pi \times 6 \times 10^{-6}}{3}$
$=36 \pi \times 10^{3} N-m^{2} / C$

question: A hollow spherical shell of radius $r$ has a uniform charge density $\sigma$. It is kept in a cube of edge $3 r$ such that the centres of the cube and the shell coincide. Then the electric flux coming out of one face of a cube is ( $\varepsilon_{0}=$ permittivity of free space)
explanation:
Let, spherical shell has charge $=q$
Change density $\quad \sigma=\dfrac{q}{A} \Rightarrow \sigma=\dfrac{q}{4 \pi r^{2}}$
$q=\sigma 4 \pi r^{2}$
flux through one face of cube;
$\phi_{E}=\dfrac{1}{6} \dfrac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}$
Put the value of
$\phi_{E}=\dfrac{1}{6} \times \dfrac{4 \pi r^{2} \sigma}{\varepsilon_{0}} \Rightarrow \phi_{E}=\dfrac{2 \pi r^{2} \sigma}{3 \varepsilon_{0}}$
Hence, the correct option is (4).

question: The electric flux from a cube of edge $l$ is $\phi$ when charge $q$ is enclosed. If the edge of the cube is made ( $2 / 3$ ) $l$ and the charge enclosed in the cube is doubled, then the electric flux value will be
explanation:
According to Gauss's law, the electric flux through a closed surface is $\phi = \frac{{{q_{{\text{net}}}}}}{{{\varepsilon _0}}} \Rightarrow {\phi _E}\,\alpha \,{q_{{\text{net}}}} \Rightarrow \frac{{{\phi _2}}}{{{\phi _1}}} = \frac{{{q_2}}}{{{q_1}}}$
Electric flux through a closed surface does not depend on the dimensions of closed surface, it depends only the amount of charge enclosed by the closed surface. given $q_{2}=2 q_{1}$
$\therefore \dfrac{\phi_{2}}{\phi_{1}}=\dfrac{2 q_{1}}{q_{1}} \Rightarrow \phi_{2}=2 \phi$

question: The electric field in a certain region is acting radially outward and is given by ${E=A r^{2}}$. A charge contained in a sphere of radius ${a}$ centred at the origin of the field, will be given by:
explanation:
Flux linked with sphere ${=\vec{E} \cdot d \vec{s}}$
Since electric field is radial, it is always perpendicular to the surface.
${\phi=A r^{2} \cdot\left(4 \pi r^{2}\right)}$
${=A\left(a^{2}\right)\left(4 \pi a^{2}\right)(r=a)}$
${=A\left(4 \pi a^{4}\right)}$
from Gauss' law,
${\phi=\dfrac{q_{\text {in }}}{\varepsilon_{.0}} \Rightarrow q_{\text {in }}=\phi \cdot \varepsilon_{0}}$
${\Rightarrow q_{\text {in }}=4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} A a^{4}}$

question: A point charge ${q}$ is located on the axis of a disc of radius ${R}$ at a distance ${b=5 \sqrt{3} {~m}}$ from the plane of the disc as shown in figure. What should be the radius of the disc  if one-fourth of the total electric flux from the charge passes through the disc.
explanation:
From the concept of solid angle the flux of $Q$ which passes through disc, $\phi=\dfrac{Q}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}(1-\cos \theta)$
It is given, $\phi=\dfrac{1}{4} \phi_{\text {total }}=\dfrac{Q}{4 \varepsilon_{0}}$
$\dfrac{Q}{4 \varepsilon_{0}}=\dfrac{Q}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}(1-\cos \theta) \Rightarrow \cos \theta=\dfrac{1}{2}$ or $\theta=60^{\circ}$
Hence, $\tan 60^{\circ}=\dfrac{R}{b}$
$\Rightarrow R=b \tan 60^{\circ}$
$ \Rightarrow R = \sqrt 3 \times 5\sqrt 3 = 15\,m$

question: A point charge ${q}$ is placed at the centre of the cubical box. If ${q=12000 \epsilon_{0} C}$, find the flux through shaded area of surface is
explanation:
Total flux coming out of the cubical box is given as
$\phi_{0}=\dfrac{q}{\epsilon_{0}}$
This total flux is emerging equally from each of the six face of the box.
Thus flux through each face can be given as
$\phi=\dfrac{q}{6 \epsilon_{0}}$
The flux through shaded portion would be one fourth of the flux through each face as through all the four triangular portions flux would be same due to symmetry, thus we have
${\phi ^\prime } = \frac{\phi }{4} = \frac{q}{{24{ \in _0}}} = \frac{{12000{ \in _0}}}{{24{ \in _0}}} = 500\,N - {m^2}/C$

question: If the electric flux entering and leaving an enclosed surface respectively are $\phi_{1}$ and $\phi_{2}$, the electric charge inside the surface will be
explanation:
Any closed surface is equal to the
net charge inside the surface divided by $\varepsilon_{0}$.
Therefore, $\phi=\dfrac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}$
Let $-q_{1}$ be the charge, due to which
flux $\phi_{1}$ is entering the surface
$\phi_{1}=\dfrac{-q_{1}}{\varepsilon_{0}}$
or$-q_{1}=\varepsilon_{0} \phi_{1}$
Let $+q_{2}$ be the charge, due to which flux $\phi_{2}$ is leaving the surface
$\therefore \quad \phi_{2}=\dfrac{q_{2}}{\varepsilon_{0}} \text { or } q_{2}=\varepsilon_{0} \phi_{2}$
So, electric charge inside the surface
$ = {q_2} - {q_1}$
$ = {\varepsilon _0}{\phi _2} + {\varepsilon _0}{\phi _1}$
$ = {\varepsilon _0}\left( {{\phi _2} + {\phi _1}} \right)$

question:
Read the Assertion and Reason carefully to mark correct option given below:

Assertion: Surface charge density represents a smoothed out average of the microscopic charge density over a macroscopic area.

Reason: Surface charge density is defined in terms of discrete charges at the microscopic level.
explanation: The assertion is true as surface charge density ( $\sigma$ ) is indeed a macroscopic average of the charge distribution, ignoring the discrete nature of charge at a microscopic level. The reason is true in the sense that the concept ofsurface charge density arises because it is impractical to work with discrete charges for macroscopic applications. However, it is not the correct explanation for the assertion. Surface charge density is a concept used to simplify the description of charge distribution at a scale where it is impractical to account for individual charges. Correct option is (2)

question: A simple pendulum consists of a small sphere of mass $m$ suspended by a thread of length $l$. The sphere carries a positive charge $q$. The pendulum is allowed to do small oscillations in uniform electric field $E$ with direction vertically upwards. The time period of oscillation is
explanation:
The given situation is shown below. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952814/preptracker/questions/q_111862_explanation_1764952814404.png Effective gravitational acceleration $g_{\text {eff }}=\left(g-\dfrac{q E}{m}\right)$
$\therefore \quad T=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g_{\text {eff }}}}=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{g-\dfrac{q E}{m}}}$

question: The vehicles carrying inflammable fluids usually have metallic chains touching the ground,
explanation:
To avoid the inflammable fluids from catching fire, metallic chains are suspended on the vehicles. Metallic chains are connected to the earth which causes earthing and prevents accidents due to sparking.
Sparking is caused by friction which induces positive charge and negative charge simultaneously on the surface of bodies in contact. Metallic chains provide a path to the charge induced on the surface of vehicle and transfer it to the earth via earthing.

options.text: to protect tyres from catching dirt from ground
to conduct excess charge due to air friction to ground and prevent sparking.

question:
Assertion: If the bob of a simple pendulum is kept in a horizontal electric field, its period of oscillation will remain same.

Reason: If bob is charged and kept in horizontal electric field, then the time period will be decreased.
explanation: When an uncharged bob of a pendulum is placed in an electric field, it does not affect its time period as no electric force can be experienced by the bob since it is neutral. So, the acceleration of the bob remains same, i.e., $g$ and hence its time period. But when a charged bob of a pendulum is placed in horizontal electric field, the bob experiences an electric force in horizontal direction due to which the effective value of increases. Hence its time period decreases.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

question:
Assertion: The lightning conductor at the top of high building has sharp pointed ends.

Reason: The surface density of charge at sharp points is very high resulting in setting up of electric wind.
explanation:
The lightning conductor at the top of a high building has sharp pointed ends. This is correct because lightning conductors often have pointed ends to facilitate the discharge of electric charges.
Reason explains why the sharp pointed ends are used. It correctly states that the surface density of charge at sharp points is very high, which results in the setting up of an electric wind. This electric wind helps in the discharge of electric charges to nearby charged clouds and reduces the chances of lightning striking the building. If lightning does strike, the charge is conducted to the earth, protecting the building.
The Reason provides a valid scientific explanation for the Assertion, so the correct choice is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.

question:
Assertion: Polarisation is not possible under action of an external field if molecules of matter are non-polar.

Reason: In presence of an external field, the polar molecules tend to align with the field and a net dipole moment results.
explanation: Without an external field, the dipole moment of different molecules in a piece of matter are randomly oriented, so there is no net total dipole moment. In the presence of an external field, the polar molecules tend to align with the field and a net dipole moment results. Non-polar  molecules undergo polarisation and develop dipole moment in presence of electric field.
